title: Enhancing Oracle Bone Inscription Recognition via Multi-Scale Layer Attention
summary: Oracle Bone Inscriptions (OBIs) recognition plays a crucial role in understanding ancient Chinese culture. However, accurately recognizing OBIs remains highly challenging due to their complex, irregular, and often degraded shapes. Traditional methods rely on expert knowledge and manual analysis, which are time-consuming and error-prone. Although deep learning has greatly advanced general image recognition, existing methods struggle to capture the fine-grained details and subtle variations inherent in OBIs, resulting in limited performance. Even most recent and effective layer attention techniques are designed to capture fine-grained dependencies through enhanced inter-layer interactions, yet they still exhibit only marginal improvements in OBIs recognition. To address these limitations, we propose Multi-Scale Layer Attention (MSLA), a novel paradigm that explicitly models both multi-scale and cross-layer feature interactions. By enriching the representation with fine-grained details across multiple spatial scales, MSLA enables more accurate and robust OBIs recognition. Extensive experiments on large-scale OBIs datasets demonstrate that MSLA consistently outperforms existing attention mechanisms while maintaining computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Multi-Scale Layer Attention (MSLA), a novel attention mechanism that explicitly models multi-scale and cross-layer feature interactions to improve recognition of complex, irregular Oracle Bone Inscriptions, consistently outperforming existing methods on large-scale datasets.
AI.motivation: Oracle Bone Inscriptions (OBIs) recognition is important for understanding ancient Chinese culture but remains highly challenging due to complex, irregular, degraded shapes. Existing deep learning methods, including recent layer attention techniques, struggle to capture fine-grained details and subtle variations in OBIs, showing only marginal improvements.
AI.method: Proposes Multi-Scale Layer Attention (MSLA), a novel paradigm that explicitly models both multi-scale and cross-layer feature interactions to enrich representations with fine-grained details across multiple spatial scales.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on large-scale OBIs datasets demonstrate that MSLA consistently outperforms existing attention mechanisms while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: MSLA enables more accurate and robust OBIs recognition compared to prior attention-based approaches, addressing a key limitation in fine-grained detail capture for complex ancient character recognition tasks.

title: Joint Medical Image Enhancement and Segmentation with Diffusion-based Symbiotic Information Interaction
summary: Image quality is critical for accurate medical diagnosis. However, MRI, CT, and ultrasound images are often of low resolution and quality due to cost constraints, complicating the visualization of key anatomical structures and lesions. While such limitations are common in practice, traditional methods treat image enhancement as a separate preprocessing step, failing to fully leverage its potential synergy with image segmentation. To address this, we propose DiSIINet (Diffusion-based Symbiotic Information Interaction Network), which is built on the principle that enhancement and segmentation should mutually reinforce each other in a unified model. Based on Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM), DiSIINet integrates an enhancement branch and a segmentation branch. These branches interact through a novel Symbiotic Information Interaction (SII) module, which facilitates dynamic, feature-level information exchange via cross-attention during the reverse diffusion process. This design enables both tasks to iteratively improve each other. The DDIM backbone ensures high-quality output and efficient inference through deterministic sampling. Experiments on multi-modal medical datasets (MRI, CT, ultrasound) show that DiSIINet achieves significant performance improvements compared to sequential or independent enhancement and segmentation approaches. The code is available at: https://github.com/Reconsider80/DiSIINet.

AI.tldr: DiSIINet is a unified diffusion-based model for medical image enhancement and segmentation that uses cross-attention modules to enable the two tasks to mutually reinforce each other, outperforming sequential or independent approaches on MRI, CT, and ultrasound datasets.
AI.motivation: Medical images (MRI, CT, ultrasound) are often low-resolution due to cost constraints, and traditional methods treat enhancement as a separate preprocessing step, failing to leverage potential synergy between enhancement and segmentation tasks.
AI.method: DiSIINet builds on Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) with two branches (enhancement and segmentation) that interact via a Symbiotic Information Interaction (SII) module using cross-attention during the reverse diffusion process, enabling dynamic feature-level exchange.
AI.result: Experiments on multi-modal medical datasets (MRI, CT, ultrasound) show significant performance improvements compared to sequential or independent enhancement and segmentation approaches.
AI.conclusion: Enhancement and segmentation should be treated as mutually reinforcing tasks in a unified model, and DiSIINet effectively demonstrates this synergy using diffusion-based architecture with cross-attention interaction.

title: Synergistic Perception-Reasoning Governance: Grounding Medical MLLMs with Verifiable Anatomical Evidence
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) show strong promise for clinical VQA and radiology report generation, yet inference-time hallucinations still undermine trustworthy use: models can produce fluent conclusions that conflict with imaging evidence. Existing mitigation strategies typically rely on additional training, external retrieval/knowledge bases, or multi-stage post-hoc verification, which increases cost and pipeline complexity and often generalizes poorly across models and tasks.To address this, we propose a holistic, training-free evidence-injection framework that systematically mitigates hallucinations through dual-side evidence injection. By leveraging ROI priors acquired using MedSAM in our implementation, we recalibrate the visual perception trajectory via ROI-guided activation modulation while anchoring the textual reasoning trajectory by mapping anatomical coordinates into discrete semantic tokens as verifiable external memory. Then we introduce a task-aware dynamic router to select modality-specific interventions based on task semantics, balancing perceptual grounding and linguistic fluency. We conduct systematic evaluations on 2 tasks and 5 datasets using \texttt{LLaVA-1.5-7B}, \texttt{LLaVA-Med-1.5-7B}, \texttt{Qwen3-VL-8B/32B}, and \texttt{InternVL-3.5-8B/38B}. Controlled ablations and visualizations further validate the framework, which consistently outperforms baselines across medical benchmarks, improving close-ended accuracy by up to $\sim\mathbf{6}\%\uparrow$ and reducing open-ended hallucinations by $\sim\mathbf{35}\%\downarrow$. The code has been made available on GitHub: \href{https://github.com/Henry991115/SPRG}{\textcolor{blue}{https://github.com/Henry991115/SPRG}}.

AI.tldr: A training-free evidence-injection framework using MedSAM-based ROI priors, activation modulation, and semantic token mapping reduces hallucinations in clinical MLLMs across 5 datasets, improving accuracy by ~6% and reducing hallucinations by ~35%.
AI.motivation: Existing hallucination mitigation methods for MLLMs in clinical settings require additional training, external knowledge bases, or complex post-hoc verification, increasing cost and limiting generalizability across models and tasks.
AI.method: Proposes a training-free dual-side evidence-injection framework: (1) ROI-guided activation modulation using MedSAM priors to recalibrate visual perception; (2) mapping anatomical coordinates into discrete semantic tokens as external memory for textual reasoning; (3) task-aware dynamic router to select modality-specific interventions balancing perceptual grounding and linguistic fluency.
AI.result: Evaluated on 2 tasks and 5 datasets across 6 model variants; improves close-ended accuracy by up to ~6% and reduces open-ended hallucinations by ~35%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed training-free framework consistently mitigates hallucinations across diverse medical MLLMs and tasks, offering a cost-effective and generalizable solution for trustworthy clinical VQA and radiology report generation.

title: Lost in the Tail: Addressing Geographic Imbalance in Urban Visual Place Recognition
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026, 28 pages including supplementary material
summary: Urban-scale Visual Place Recognition (VPR) aims to identify the geographic location of a query image by matching it against a geo-tagged database. While recent methods achieve impressive performance, they overlook a serious long-tailed problem hidden in urban-scale datasets, which biases the model towards locations with abundant images and ignores less-visited areas, causing models to systematically favor frequently photographed locations while failing in sparsely covered areas. In this paper, we systematically characterize this imbalance challenge and propose Distribution-Aware Place Recognition (DAPR), a model-agnostic plug-in framework that rebalances gradient contributions across head and tail classes. Additionally, within classification-retrieval pipelines, DAPR applies a multi-scale distance search mechanism to compute per-class distributional compactness, providing complementary gains at the retrieval stage. On the large-scale SF-XL benchmark, our framework outperforms the previous classification-retrieval baseline by 18.3% on test set v1, and 6.7% on test set v2. As a plug-in module, it achieves consistent improvements across representative VPR methods on SF-XL, MSLS, and Pitts30k, demonstrating broad generalizability across different methods and benchmarks.

AI.tldr: DAPR is a plug-in framework that addresses the long-tailed distribution problem in urban-scale visual place recognition by rebalancing gradient contributions and using multi-scale distance search, achieving significant improvements across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing VPR methods overlook the long-tailed problem in urban-scale datasets, where models are biased toward locations with abundant images while failing in sparsely covered areas, leading to systematic failure in less-visited locations.
AI.method: DAPR is a model-agnostic plug-in framework that (1) rebalances gradient contributions across head and tail classes in classification-retrieval pipelines, and (2) applies a multi-scale distance search mechanism to compute per-class distributional compactness for complementary gains at retrieval stage.
AI.result: Outperforms previous baseline by 18.3% on SF-XL v1 and 6.7% on v2. Shows consistent improvements across SF-XL, MSLS, and Pitts30k benchmarks with various VPR methods.
AI.conclusion: DAPR effectively addresses the long-tailed distribution challenge in urban-scale VPR, demonstrating broad generalizability across different methods and benchmarks as a plug-in module.

title: PixelEyes: Decoupling Perception and Reasoning for Pinpoint Visual Evidence Seeking
summary: This paper explores multi-turn visual reasoning and observes that MLLMs repeatedly fail to localize the target, leading to long, redundant trajectories. We attribute this failure to the entanglement of reasoning and perception within a single model, the MLLM reasons and localizes simultaneously, and inaccurate localization triggers additional reasoning turns that bloat the trajectory. To solve this problem, we propose PixelEyes, a multi-turn visual reasoning agent that explicitly decouples reasoning from perception, i.e., the reasoner decides what to look for, while a specialized perception tool answers where it is. Specifically, PixelEyes introduces 1) Mask-guided Visual Search. A referring segmentation model is invoked to provide mask-precise localization, freeing the reasoner from the need to compensate for imprecise grounding. 2) Semantic-region Breadth-first Search (BFS). To eliminate redundant loops caused by repeatedly cropping incorrect sub-regions, we organize exploration as a breadth-first search over semantic regions. To internalize these capabilities, we construct the PixelEyes-6K dataset by resynthesizing expert trajectories from existing data. This explicitly embeds our mask-guided search and BFS logic into the model. We further introduce Pinpoint-Bench, a zero-hint visual search benchmark, i.e., no location cues are provided in the question, with instance-level masks and bounding boxes that separate localization failures from reasoning failures, enabling fine-grained analysis of failure modes such as inattentional blindness. Recent state-of-the-art MLLMs and visual reasoning agents leave large headroom on Pinpoint-Bench, demonstrating its quality and difficulty. Code and models are open-sourced.

AI.tldr: PixelEyes decouples reasoning from perception in MLLMs using mask-guided visual search and semantic-region BFS to eliminate redundant localization failures, achieving strong results on the new Pinpoint-Bench benchmark.
AI.motivation: MLLMs fail at multi-turn visual reasoning because reasoning and perception are entangled in a single model—inaccurate localization triggers redundant reasoning turns, bloating trajectories.
AI.method: 1) Mask-guided Visual Search: uses a referring segmentation model for precise mask localization. 2) Semantic-region Breadth-first Search (BFS): organizes exploration as BFS over semantic regions to avoid redundant cropping loops. 3) PixelEyes-6K dataset: resynthesizes expert trajectories to embed these capabilities.
AI.result: PixelEyes outperforms recent MLLMs and visual reasoning agents on Pinpoint-Bench, a new zero-hint visual search benchmark with instance-level masks/bboxes for fine-grained failure analysis.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling reasoning from perception with specialized tools (mask-guided search + BFS) effectively resolves redundant reasoning loops; Pinpoint-Bench reveals significant headroom for future work.

title: Segmenting, Fast and Slow: Real-Time Open-Vocabulary Video Instance Segmentation with Dual-Path Processing
summary: Object-centric models inspired by DETR have become the dominant paradigm for open-vocabulary video instance segmentation (OV-VIS). While recent efforts have reduced the computational cost of pixel decoding, textual modality fusion, and object decoding to make these architectures more suitable for mobile devices, real-time on-device inference at high frame rates remains an open challenge. In this paper, we introduce SegFS, a dual-stream fast-slow framework that significantly improves efficiency without sacrificing accuracy. On sparse keyframes, an open-vocabulary object-based model predicts instance-level representations. These representations are then projected back into the backbone feature space to condition a lightweight fast network, which efficiently relocalizes and segments the instances in subsequent frames. By shifting instance propagation from object decoding to feature-space conditioning, our approach decouples multimodal semantic understanding from dense mask prediction and enables efficient temporal propagation. The proposed fast branch achieves up to 14x lower latency than the mobile-oriented MOBIUS model, while maintaining competitive segmentation performance on standard OV-VIS benchmarks.

AI.tldr: SegFS introduces a dual-stream fast-slow framework for open-vocabulary video instance segmentation that projects instance representations from sparse keyframes into backbone feature space to condition a lightweight fast network, achieving up to 14x lower latency than MOBIUS while maintaining competitive performance.
AI.motivation: Real-time on-device inference for OV-VIS at high frame rates remains an open challenge despite recent efficiency improvements in mobile-oriented architectures.
AI.method: A dual-stream fast-slow framework where: (1) a slow stream processes sparse keyframes with an open-vocabulary object-based model to predict instance-level representations, (2) these representations are projected back into backbone feature space to condition a lightweight fast network, (3) the fast network relocalizes and segments instances in subsequent frames, decoupling multimodal semantic understanding from dense mask prediction.
AI.result: Up to 14x lower latency than the mobile-oriented MOBIUS model, with competitive segmentation performance on standard OV-VIS benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Shifting instance propagation from object decoding to feature-space conditioning effectively enables efficient temporal propagation while maintaining accuracy, making real-time on-device OV-VIS more feasible.

title: Decompose, Compare, and Decide: Multimodal LLMs are Implicit Few-Shot Learners
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable abilities when analyzing images, yet translating these capabilities to few-shot image classification remains challenging. To bridge this gap, we present DeCoDe, a simple yet effective technique that enables off-the-shelf MLLMs to act as strong few-shot classifiers without any additional training. Our approach builds on the idea of few-shot classification as a set of pairwise image comparisons, decomposing the task into a set of binary decisions. Given a query image and a support image from a candidate class, the MLLM is prompted to decide whether the two images depict the same class. The logit corresponding to an affirmative response is then used as a similarity score to assign the query image to the most likely class. While this already yields good results, we show that providing additional high-level information, such as the data domain, to the model further improves performance. Our evaluation provides an extensive analysis of various inference variants on a suite of twelve datasets, six established and six newly curated few-shot benchmarks spanning across diverse domains. The results show that the proposed simple decomposition technique can turn off-the-shelf MLLMs into powerful few-shot learners, significantly outperforming current state-of-the-art few-shot methods on both standard and novel domains. Code is available at https://github.com/yunhanwang1105/DeCoDe.

AI.tldr: DeCoDe enables off-the-shelf MLLMs to perform few-shot classification by decomposing the task into binary image comparisons, using affirmative logits as similarity scores, outperforming state-of-the-art methods without additional training.
AI.motivation: MLLMs excel at image analysis but struggle with few-shot image classification; there is a gap between their capabilities and effective few-shot learning that the authors aim to bridge without additional training.
AI.method: DeCoDe decomposes few-shot classification into pairwise binary decisions: for each query image and support image pair, the MLLM judges if they share the same class. The logit of an affirmative response serves as a similarity score to classify the query. Additional high-level information (e.g., data domain) is also provided to improve performance.
AI.result: Evaluated on twelve datasets (six established + six newly curated few-shot benchmarks), DeCoDe significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art few-shot methods across both standard and novel domains.
AI.conclusion: Simple task decomposition can turn off-the-shelf MLLMs into powerful few-shot learners without any training, offering a strong baseline for future research.

title: A Synthetic-Driven Vision System for Assembly Step Recognition
summary: Quality control in industrial assembly is essential, and real-time monitoring of the assembly process is crucial for preventing costly defects and ensuring production reliability. Vision-based automated inspection offers a powerful solution for such real-time monitoring. However, due to the specialized industrial components and processes, training these models typically relies on task-specific real-world data, which is costly and labor-intensive to collect and annotate. In this paper, we propose a system that automatically generates realistic assembly sequences and further trains real-time inspection models using the synthetic data. It can be efficiently applied to a given task within an hour, requiring only CAD models and simple step descriptions. Focusing on practical challenges, our system integrates a physics-based motion generation module to capture the variance of different human assembly, designs domain-randomized rendering to deal with the environmental complexity and variation, and employs an object-detection-based step recognition module for robust sim-to-real transfer, leading to 92.4% accuracy on a real-world assembly case with 46.7%, 15.8% and 61.2% performance improvement, respectively. Overall, our system provides a practical solution for industrial assembly inspection without requiring expensive real-world data collection and annotation, with the effectiveness validated on real industrial assembly tasks.

AI.tldr: A system that generates synthetic assembly sequences from CAD models to train real-time vision-based inspection models, achieving 92.4% accuracy on real-world tasks without real data collection.
AI.motivation: Real-time monitoring is critical for quality control in industrial assembly, but training vision models requires costly and labor-intensive real-world data collection and annotation.
AI.method: Three components: (1) physics-based motion generation for human assembly variance, (2) domain-randomized rendering for environmental variation, (3) object-detection-based step recognition for robust sim-to-real transfer.
AI.result: 92.4% accuracy on real-world assembly case; 46.7%, 15.8%, and 61.2% improvement from each respective module.
AI.conclusion: The system provides a practical, efficient (within an hour per task) solution for industrial assembly inspection using only CAD models and step descriptions, eliminating expensive real-world data needs.

title: MG-SpaIR: Multi-grade Sparse-guided Implicit Representation for Training-Data-Free Image Restoration
summary: MG-SpaIR is a training-data-free framework for restoring a clean image from a single observation corrupted by a mixture of blur, downsampling, noise, and missing pixels. Building on implicit neural representations (INRs), we introduce a multi-grade coarse-to-fine residual hierarchy that progressively refines the reconstruction across resolution grades, improving representational fidelity and mitigating spectral limitations. To stabilize reconstruction optimization and suppress INR-induced artifacts, we further propose an explicit sparse proximal regularization (e.g., $\ell_0$-type) applied directly in the high-resolution image domain, which discourages spurious high-frequency patterns while preserving sharp structures. The resulting optimization is solved efficiently via a multi-grade proximal alternating scheme, and we establish convergence guarantees for the associated updates under standard regularity conditions. Experiments on mixed-degradation benchmarks demonstrate that MG-SpaIR consistently outperforms strong training-data-free baselines such as Deep Image Prior, providing a stable, interpretable, and data-efficient alternative to conventional learning-based restoration methods.

AI.tldr: MG-SpaIR is a training-data-free framework that restores clean images from mixed degradations (blur, downsampling, noise, missing pixels) using multi-grade implicit neural representations with sparse proximal regularization, outperforming methods like Deep Image Prior.
AI.motivation: To develop a training-data-free image restoration method that handles complex mixed degradations without requiring large datasets, while overcoming spectral limitations and artifacts inherent in standard implicit neural representations.
AI.method: A multi-grade coarse-to-fine residual hierarchy built on implicit neural representations, combined with explicit sparse proximal regularization (ℓ₀-type) applied in the high-resolution domain, optimized via a multi-grade proximal alternating scheme with convergence guarantees.
AI.result: MG-SpaIR consistently outperforms strong training-data-free baselines (e.g., Deep Image Prior) on mixed-degradation benchmarks, providing stable and interpretable restoration.
AI.conclusion: MG-SpaIR offers a stable, interpretable, and data-efficient alternative to conventional learning-based methods for mixed-degradation image restoration.

title: A Mechanism-Driven Theory of Phase Transitions in Active Learning
summary: Active learning (AL) performance is known to be budget-dependent, yet regimes are typically defined by heuristic label counts that fail to generalize across datasets or architectures. We characterize AL dynamics by reframing budget regimes as shifts in the dominant generalization mechanism. By reinterpreting PAC-style risk components as dynamic interacting terms, we prove that dominance shifts are structurally unavoidable, creating a moving bottleneck for generalization. We operationalize this using measurable proxies and a segmented regression procedure to identify a tripartite taxonomy: data-driven, transition, and model-driven phases. Our framework explains the long-standing observation that representativeness, coverage, and uncertainty strategies excel at different stages. Experiments across natural and medical imaging show that AL efficiency depends on the alignment between the strategy's inductive bias and the active bottleneck. Moreover, self-supervised representation shift transitions earlier along the labeling trajectory, highlighting the role of representation quality in shaping AL dynamics. Overall, this work provides a unified framework for the next generation of transition-aware AL algorithms.

AI.tldr: This paper characterizes active learning budget regimes by reframing them as shifts in dominant generalization mechanisms, proving these shifts are structurally unavoidable. It introduces a tripartite taxonomy (data-driven, transition, model-driven phases) using measurable proxies and segmented regression, explaining why different AL strategies excel at different stages and showing that AL efficiency depends on aligning inductive bias with the active bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Active learning performance is budget-dependent, but existing budget regimes are defined by heuristic label counts that fail to generalize across datasets or architectures. The authors aim to provide a principled, unified framework for understanding AL dynamics across different budget stages.
AI.method: The authors reinterpret PAC-style risk components as dynamic interacting terms, prove dominance shifts are structurally unavoidable, and operationalize this via measurable proxies and segmented regression to identify a tripartite taxonomy (data-driven, transition, model-driven phases). Experiments are conducted on natural and medical imaging datasets.
AI.result: AL efficiency depends on alignment between a strategy's inductive bias and the active bottleneck. Self-supervised representation shift transitions earlier along the labeling trajectory, highlighting representation quality's role in shaping AL dynamics. The framework explains why representativeness, coverage, and uncertainty strategies excel at different stages.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a unified framework for the next generation of transition-aware AL algorithms, offering both theoretical grounding and practical tools for understanding budget-dependent AL behavior.

title: Progressive Pose-Guided 4D Animal Reconstruction from Monocular Video
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Camera-ready author version
summary: Reconstructing 4D animals from monocular videos is challenging due to large inter-species variation, complex articulations, and the lack of reliable templates. Existing approaches typically rely on either strict category-specific priors that restrict generalization, or unconstrained generative models that sacrifice input fidelity. To bridge this gap, we present a progressive test-time optimization framework built on 3D Gaussian Splatting for high-fidelity 4D animal reconstruction from a single video. Our key insight is that a coarse shape prior suffices when coupled with a progressive strategy that disentangles articulated pose from non-rigid deformation. Specifically, we employ a symmetry-aware temporal encoding that exploits bilateral cues while absorbing camera estimation drift and a part-conditioned deformation mechanism guided by learnable part anchors and a learnable skinning field. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach generalizes robustly across diverse species, achieving superior geometric accuracy, temporal consistency, and visual fidelity compared to existing baselines, even under severe prior mismatch.

AI.tldr: A progressive test-time optimization framework using 3D Gaussian Splatting for high-fidelity 4D animal reconstruction from monocular videos, employing symmetry-aware temporal encoding and part-conditioned deformation without requiring strict category-specific priors.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for 4D animal reconstruction either rely on strict category-specific priors (limiting generalization) or use unconstrained generative models (sacrificing input fidelity). The authors aim to bridge this gap with a method that generalizes across diverse species while maintaining high reconstruction fidelity.
AI.method: A progressive test-time optimization framework built on 3D Gaussian Splatting, using: (1) a coarse shape prior, (2) symmetry-aware temporal encoding to exploit bilateral cues and absorb camera drift, (3) part-conditioned deformation mechanism guided by learnable part anchors and a learnable skinning field, and (4) a progressive strategy disentangling articulated pose from non-rigid deformation.
AI.result: The approach generalizes robustly across diverse species, achieving superior geometric accuracy, temporal consistency, and visual fidelity compared to existing baselines, even under severe prior mismatch.
AI.conclusion: A coarse shape prior combined with progressive optimization and disentangled pose/non-rigid deformation modeling is sufficient for high-quality 4D animal reconstruction from monocular video, outperforming existing category-specific and unconstrained generative approaches.

title: Steal the Patch Size: Adversarially Manipulate Vision-Language Models
summary: We present a black-box model-stealing attack that recovers private vision-tokenizer configurations of deployed vision-language models (VLMs), including the visual patch size and input preprocessing pipeline. The key idea is a task-level side channel induced by ViT-style patchification: when a synthetic grid image is aligned with the hidden patch grid, boundary cues are erased at tokenization, causing periodic accuracy drop. By sweeping the grid cell size and measuring these collapses, we infer the patch size; by introducing padding and a consistency-check test, we further identify whether preprocessing is dynamic- or fixed-resolution and recover the target resize resolution. Across open-source Qwen-VL variants and proprietary models including GPT and Claude, we reliably recover tokenizer-related parameters. Finally, we show that such leakage enables preprocessing-aware transfer attacks and model-targeted adversarial manipulation.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a black-box attack that steals private vision-tokenizer configurations (patch size, preprocessing pipeline) from VLMs by exploiting a side channel from ViT patchification, enabling preprocessing-aware transfer attacks and targeted adversarial manipulation.
AI.motivation: To expose the vulnerability of vision-tokenizer parameters in deployed VLMs and demonstrate that these seemingly minor hyperparameters can be stolen and exploited for downstream attacks.
AI.method: The attack uses synthetic grid images; when the grid cell size aligns with the hidden patch size, periodic accuracy drops occur due to erased boundary cues at tokenization. Padding and consistency tests further identify whether preprocessing is dynamic- or fixed-resolution and recover the target resize resolution.
AI.result: The attack reliably recovers patch size, preprocessing type (dynamic vs. fixed), and resize resolution across open-source (Qwen-VL) and proprietary models (GPT, Claude).
AI.conclusion: Stolen tokenizer configurations enable stronger transfer attacks and model-targeted adversarial examples, highlighting a new side-channel threat for VLMs.

title: PRISM-VO: Scale-Aware Visual Odometry Using Photometric Plenoptic Bundle Adjustment
comment: Accepted for publication at the 19th European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV) 2026
summary:
We introduce PRISM-VO, a novel pure optimization-based sparse photometric visual odometry framework for focused plenoptic cameras. The core of PRISM-VO is a novel photometric plenoptic bundle adjustment which jointly optimizes camera poses and inverse depth values of points in a sliding window. By combining geometric depth from a single plenoptic image with temporal multi-view constraints, PRISM-VO achieves accurate and drift-resilient motion estimation. Through explicit modeling of the plenoptic projection, PRISM-VO provides reliable metric-scale reconstructions, overcoming the scale ambiguity of monocular SLAM algorithms. Importantly, our approach relies solely on a single plenoptic sensor and avoids complex initialization, as depth priors are computed directly from plenoptic imaging.
  Experiments show that PRISM-VO outperforms the current state-of-the-art plenoptic visual odometry method on indoor and outdoor scenes. The proposed approach rivals other optimization- and learning-based methods while accurately and reliably recovering a metric scale of the scene.
  Project page: https://prism-vo.github.io/

AI.tldr: PRISM-VO is a pure optimization-based sparse photometric visual odometry framework for focused plenoptic cameras that jointly optimizes camera poses and inverse depths in a sliding window, achieving accurate metric-scale motion estimation without scale ambiguity and outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: To overcome the scale ambiguity of monocular SLAM and the complexity of initialization in existing methods by leveraging plenoptic cameras that provide direct depth priors from a single image, enabling accurate and drift-resilient metric-scale motion estimation.
AI.method: A pure optimization-based sparse photometric visual odometry framework using a novel photometric plenoptic bundle adjustment that jointly optimizes camera poses and inverse depth values in a sliding window, combining geometric depth from single plenoptic images with temporal multi-view constraints, with explicit modeling of plenoptic projection.
AI.result: Outperforms current state-of-the-art plenoptic visual odometry on both indoor and outdoor scenes, rivals other optimization- and learning-based methods, and reliably recovers metric scale.
AI.conclusion: PRISM-VO demonstrates that a pure optimization-based approach using only a single plenoptic sensor can achieve accurate metric-scale visual odometry without complex initialization, setting a new state-of-the-art for plenoptic VO.

title: DriftScope: Measuring The Hidden Effects of Diffusion Model Adaptation
summary: Adapting pre-trained text-to-image diffusion models, whether to learn new visual concepts or erase unwanted ones, is routinely evaluated on its intended effects alone. We argue this framing is incomplete. Through sparse autoencoder analysis and zero-shot classification, we demonstrate that adaptation systematically damages semantically unrelated concepts in ways that aggregate metrics structurally cannot surface: when damage is severe enough for FID and KID to respond, the model is already nearly unusable; when the model remains functional, FID and KID stay flat while specific classes silently suffer worst-case zero-shot accuracy drops of up to 18.9 points and concept-level distributions shift dramatically. This pattern appears at both ends of the adaptation spectrum (concept customization and concept unlearning), suggesting it is a systematic consequence of weight-level modification rather than an artifact of any particular method. To surface this hidden drift before deployment, we introduce DriftScope, a prompt-level diagnostic tool that takes any two model checkpoints and returns a ranked list of tokens whose visual concepts have shifted most between them. DriftScope optimizes a soft prompt to attribute drift at the token level without requiring access to real data or model internals. The result is an interpretable, concept-level audit that aggregate evaluation cannot provide.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that adapting pre-trained text-to-image diffusion models (for concept learning or unlearning) causes systematic, silent damage to unrelated semantic concepts that aggregate metrics like FID/KID fail to detect. The authors introduce DriftScope, a prompt-level diagnostic tool that identifies which visual concepts have shifted most between model checkpoints.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of model adaptation focuses only on intended effects (learning or erasing specific concepts), ignoring unintended damage to semantically unrelated concepts. Aggregate metrics (FID, KID) are insensitive to subtle but severe concept-level degradation, leaving hidden drift undetected before deployment.
AI.method: The authors use sparse autoencoder analysis and zero-shot classification to demonstrate hidden drift. They then propose DriftScope, which optimizes a soft prompt to attribute drift at the token level between two model checkpoints, requiring no real data or model internals access.
AI.result: Adaptation causes worst-case zero-shot accuracy drops of up to 18.9 points and dramatic concept-level distribution shifts, even when FID/KID remain flat. This pattern holds across both concept customization and unlearning. FID/KID only respond when the model is nearly unusable.
AI.conclusion: Weight-level modification in diffusion model adaptation systematically damages unrelated concepts, which aggregate metrics cannot surface. DriftScope provides an interpretable, concept-level audit to detect this hidden drift before deployment.

title: VOCA: Visual Odometry with Codec Awareness
summary: Camera pose estimation from image streams is a critical component of spatial world models that integrate perception into planning and decision-making. Nearly all Visual Odometry (VO) and Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (V-SLAM) systems have focused on datasets containing raw, uncompressed videos. Many working systems instead use ubiquitous hardware units to efficiently compress and decode video streams, saving orders of magnitude in storage and bandwidth. However, this lossy compression introduces visual artifacts that hinder the performance of traditional tracking systems. We present VOCA, a causal stereo visual-odometry method that exploits codec information to improve tracking performance. We achieve state-of-the-art performance on causal VO for relative trajectory error, efficiency, and absolute trajectory error on compressed streams. This work highlights the potential of leveraging widely available video codec information for vision tasks.

AI.tldr: VOCA is a causal stereo visual-odometry method that leverages video codec information to improve tracking performance on compressed video streams, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Existing VO and V-SLAM systems focus on raw uncompressed videos, but real-world systems use lossy video compression for efficiency. The visual artifacts from compression degrade traditional tracking performance, motivating a method that exploits codec information to maintain accuracy.
AI.method: VOCA is a causal stereo visual-odometry approach that incorporates video codec information into the tracking pipeline to improve robustness against compression artifacts.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on causal VO for relative trajectory error, efficiency, and absolute trajectory error on compressed video streams.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging widely available video codec information can significantly benefit vision tasks like visual odometry, opening new directions for robust perception in practical compressed video systems.

title: Trust the Prior (or Not): Uncertainty-Aware Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm Segmentation
summary: Robust segmentation of intraluminal thrombus is critical for risk assessment in Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm, yet it remains challenging due to heterogeneous thrombus features and low contrast with surrounding non-enhanced tissues. Domain shifts induced by different Computed Tomography Angiography (CTA) protocols further inhibit multi-center generalization of deep learning models. To address these challenges, we propose a patient-specific framework that integrates discriminative learning with anatomically informed priors. Our approach introduces two key components: (1) a patient-specific intensity normalization based on a Gaussian Mixture Model of local anatomy, and (2) an Uncertainty-Gated Anatomical Attention module that incorporates spatial priors while adaptively modulating their influence according to voxel-wise confidence. This design allows for anatomical guidance in ambiguous regions while suppressing unreliable priors. The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on in-distribution test data and substantially outperforms existing alternatives in generalization to external multi-center CTA data, while remaining interpretable through an explicit separation of visual and anatomical evidence.

AI.tldr: A patient-specific segmentation framework using GMM-based intensity normalization and uncertainty-gated anatomical attention to improve thrombus segmentation in CTA across multi-center data.
AI.motivation: Robust thrombus segmentation is critical for AAA risk assessment but faces challenges from heterogeneous features, low contrast, and domain shifts across different CTA protocols, limiting multi-center generalization of deep learning models.
AI.method: Proposes two key components: (1) patient-specific intensity normalization using a Gaussian Mixture Model of local anatomy, and (2) an Uncertainty-Gated Anatomical Attention module that incorporates spatial priors with voxel-wise confidence modulation.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on in-distribution data and substantially outperforms existing alternatives in generalization to external multi-center CTA data.
AI.conclusion: The method is effective and interpretable, explicitly separating visual and anatomical evidence for robust thrombus segmentation across diverse clinical settings.

title: EgoSafetyBench: A Diagnostic Egocentric Video Benchmark for Evaluating Embodied VLMs as Runtime Safety Guards
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are now proposed as runtime safety guards for embodied agents in homes and factories. A deployable guard must catch genuinely unsafe situations while avoiding unnecessary intervention on routine but superficially alarming activity, a distinction that binary safety benchmarks obscure. We introduce EgoSafetyBench, an egocentric video benchmark of 1,200 robot-view scenarios annotated at half-second granularity, to evaluate VLMs as streaming guards across two tracks. The situational track (800 scenarios) spans four families, from routine and safe-but-suspicious scenes to obvious and contextual hazards. The visual-channel track (400 scenarios) targets in-scene text-a sign, sticker, or label visible in the scene-that can misrepresent the physical situation, pairing each misleading sign with a truthful version to test both whether a guard flags the text as misleading and whether the text corrupts its physical-safety judgment. Both tracks use contrastive ladders: near-identical scenarios differing only in a single visible deciding cue, so a correct call must hinge on that cue rather than the overall scene type. We evaluate ten open- and closed-source VLMs. We find that while guards reliably recognize videos containing hazards, they often miss specific hazardous moments, particularly contextual hazards. Furthermore, misleading in-scene signs degrade all tested guards: vulnerable models miss up to a third of hazards, while robust models over-intervene on safe content. Matched controls reveal that apparent safety robustness often reflects indiscriminate alarming rather than true physical reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EgoSafetyBench, a fine-grained egocentric video benchmark for evaluating VLMs as runtime safety guards in embodied agents, revealing that current models struggle with detecting contextual hazards and are easily misled by in-scene text signs.
AI.motivation: Existing binary safety benchmarks fail to distinguish genuinely unsafe situations from routine but superficially alarming activity, motivating the need for more granular, temporally-annotated safety evaluation benchmarks.
AI.method: EgoSafetyBench includes 1,200 egocentric robot-view scenarios annotated at half-second granularity, with two tracks: a situational track (800 scenarios across four safety families) and a visual-channel track (400 scenarios with misleading/truthful in-scene text). Both tracks use contrastive ladders to isolate single deciding cues.
AI.result: VLMs reliably recognize videos containing hazards but often miss specific hazardous moments, especially contextual hazards. Misleading in-scene signs degrade all tested models: vulnerable models miss up to a third of hazards, robust models over-intervene. Apparent robustness often reflects indiscriminate alarming rather than true reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Current VLMs are insufficient as reliable safety guards for embodied agents, particularly in handling contextual hazards and resisting misleading environmental text, calling for better temporal grounding and reasoning capabilities.

title: Does Your ViT Still Need U-Net for Segmentation?
summary: Medical image segmentation is dominated by U-Net-style encoder-decoder architectures. Vision Transformers (ViTs) overcome the limited receptive field of convolutional networks through self-attention, enabling modeling of long-range dependencies. Early ViT-based segmentation methods typically retained U-Net-style decoders because pretrained ViT representations were insufficient to support accurate dense prediction. Recent advances in large-scale pretraining have redefined the representation capability of ViTs, reducing the reliance on U-Net-style decoder architectures in modern vision models. This prompts two questions: Is the U-Net paradigm still necessary for medical image segmentation? If not, how should an encoder-only segmentation framework be designed? Motivated by these questions, we explore key architectural choices for encoder-only medical image segmentation based on modern ViT backbones and establish a query-based encoder-only design with multi-level query modeling and learnable block fusion, realized in Encoder-only Segmentation (EoSeg). Extensive experiments across seven benchmark datasets spanning CT, MRI, histopathology, endoscopy, and dermoscopy validate the effectiveness of the proposed design across diverse medical imaging modalities, including mDice scores of 85.50% on Synapse, 91.73% on ACDC, and 93.27% on GlaS. The results demonstrate that a U-Net-style decoder is no longer necessary for medical image segmentation with modern ViT backbones and further show that EoSeg provides an effective encoder-only design. Code is available at: https://github.com/Retinal-Research/EoSeg

AI.tldr: This paper challenges the necessity of U-Net-style decoders in medical image segmentation by proposing EoSeg, an encoder-only framework using modern Vision Transformers with multi-level query modeling and learnable block fusion, achieving strong results across seven medical imaging datasets.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether the U-Net-style decoder paradigm is still necessary for medical image segmentation given the advances in large-scale pretrained Vision Transformers, and if not, how an encoder-only segmentation framework should be designed.
AI.method: The authors propose EoSeg (Encoder-only Segmentation), a query-based encoder-only design with multi-level query modeling and learnable block fusion, built on modern ViT backbones, removing the need for a U-Net-style decoder.
AI.result: Validated on seven benchmark datasets across CT, MRI, histopathology, endoscopy, and dermoscopy, achieving mDice scores of 85.50% on Synapse, 91.73% on ACDC, and 93.27% on GlaS, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance without a U-Net decoder.
AI.conclusion: A U-Net-style decoder is no longer necessary for medical image segmentation when using modern ViT backbones, and EoSeg provides an effective encoder-only alternative.

title: Leveraging Phase Information to Boost Unrolled Network Learning for Image Deblurring
summary: While most image deblurring techniques directly restore the spatial image variable, we propose an amplitude and phase decomposition recognizing the importance of accurate phase estimation in recovering sharp image details. To that end, we first develop novel linear minimum mean squared (LMMSE) estimators of the amplitude and phase of the blurred, noisy image observation. An iterative optimization algorithm follows that recovers the sharp image using the aforementioned LMMSE estimators. Finally, matrix parameters that are statistically determined and fixed in the iterative algorithm are now learned using a training dataset of clean and degraded observations. Our deblurring engine is dubbed UPADNet (Unrolled Phase and Amplitude Decomposition Network), such that each iteration of the underlying phase and amplitude recovery algorithm is parameterized and trained end-to-end. Experiments over benchmark evaluation datasets such as GoPro, RealBlur and COCO datasets confirm that UPADNet outperforms state of the art deep networks including those based on algorithm unrolling in the image domain. The benefits of UPADNet are even more pronounced in high noise and limited training data regimes.

AI.tldr: UPADNet unrolls an iterative phase-amplitude decomposition algorithm into a deep network, achieving state-of-the-art deblurring, especially under high noise and limited data.
AI.motivation: Accurate phase estimation is critical for sharp image details, but most deblurring methods directly restore spatial variables without explicitly modeling phase and amplitude.
AI.method: 1) Develop LMMSE estimators for amplitude and phase of blurred/noisy images. 2) Iterative optimization algorithm using these estimators. 3) Unroll iterations into a network (UPADNet) with learned parameters trained end-to-end.
AI.result: Outperforms SOTA deep networks (including unrolling-based methods) on GoPro, RealBlur, COCO; advantages are greater under high noise and limited training data.
AI.conclusion: Explicit phase-amplitude decomposition with algorithm unrolling yields superior deblurring performance, particularly in challenging noise/data regimes.

title: Multi-Hypothesis Test-Time Adaptation to Mitigate Underspecification
comment: 26 pages, 4 figures, 12 tables, Accepted in ECCV'26
summary: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) seeks to improve model robustness under distribution shifts by adapting parameters using unlabeled target data. However, in the absence of supervision, entropy-based adaptation is fundamentally underconstrained: multiple distinct parameter updates can achieve similarly low entropy while inducing drastically different decision boundaries. This phenomenon, known as underspecification, renders standard TTA brittle and prone to collapse into spurious modes. In this work, we reinterpret TTA through a posterior-inspired lens induced by entropy minimization, where low-entropy solutions define a pseudo-likelihood over parameters. Instead of committing to a single point estimate, we introduce a particle-based diversification framework that explores multiple plausible adaptation trajectories simultaneously. Our method can be viewed as a structured exploration of multiple plausible adaptation solutions, implemented through multi-level diversification at the output, parameter, optimizer, and input levels. Crucially, the framework acts as a plug-and-play wrapper compatible with existing TTA methods. Extensive experiments on challenging benchmarks demonstrate consistent gains in stability and robustness, achieving improvements of 3-4% under mixed shifts, 2-3% with batch size one, and 1-2.5% under label shifts, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. Our results suggest that treating TTA as a multi-hypothesis inference problem, rather than a single-point optimization task, is key to mitigating underspecification and enabling reliable real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a particle-based diversification framework for Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) that explores multiple adaptation trajectories simultaneously to mitigate the underspecification problem in entropy-based adaptation, achieving consistent improvements across various distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Standard entropy-based TTA suffers from underspecification, where multiple distinct parameter updates can achieve similarly low entropy but lead to drastically different decision boundaries, causing brittleness and collapse into spurious modes.
AI.method: A posterior-inspired particle-based diversification framework that explores multiple plausible adaptation trajectories simultaneously through multi-level diversification at the output, parameter, optimizer, and input levels. It acts as a plug-and-play wrapper compatible with existing TTA methods.
AI.result: Achieves 3-4% improvement under mixed shifts, 2-3% with batch size one, and 1-2.5% under label shifts, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines with consistent gains in stability and robustness.
AI.conclusion: Treating TTA as a multi-hypothesis inference problem rather than a single-point optimization task is key to mitigating underspecification and enabling reliable real-world deployment.

title: MVDGC: Joint 3D and 2D Multi-view Pedestrian Detection via Dual Geometric Constraints
summary: The core challenge in multi-view pedestrian detection (MVPD) lies in effective aggregation of visual features from different viewpoints for robust occlusion reasoning. Recent approaches have addressed this by first projecting image-view features onto a Bird's Eye View (BEV) map, where ground localization is then performed. Despite impressive performance, the perspective transformation induces severe distortion, causing spatial structure break and degrading the quality of object feature extraction. The blurred and ambiguous features hinder accurate BEV point localization, especially in densely populated regions. Moreover, the strong mutual relationship between the BEV ground point and image bounding boxes is not capitalized on. Although multi-view consistency of 2D detections can serve as a powerful constraint in BEV space, these detections are commonly treated as auxiliary signals rather than being jointly optimized with the primary task.In this work, we propose \textbf{MVDGC}, a unified framework that \emph{jointly estimates pedestrian locations on the BEV plane and 2D bounding boxes in image views}. MVDGC employs a \emph{sparse set of 3D cylindrical queries} that embraces geometric context across both BEV and image views, enforcing dual spatial constraints for precise localization. Specifically, the geometric constraints is established by modeling each pedestrian as a vertical cylinder whose center lies on the BEV plane and whose projection casts a rectangular box in the image views. These queries function as shape anchors that directly extract 2D features from the intact image-view features using camera projection, eliminating projection-induced distortions. The 3D cylindrical query enables the unification of BEV and ImV localization into a single task: 3D cylinder position and shape refinement. Code is available at: https://github.com/UARK-AICV/MVDGC

AI.tldr: MVDGC is a unified framework for multi-view pedestrian detection that jointly estimates pedestrian locations on BEV planes and 2D bounding boxes using sparse 3D cylindrical queries, avoiding projection-induced distortions by directly extracting features from intact image views.
AI.motivation: Existing MVPD methods suffer from perspective transformation distortion when projecting image features to BEV, causing spatial structure break and poor feature quality in dense regions. Additionally, multi-view 2D detection consistency is underutilized as it's treated as auxiliary rather than jointly optimized.
AI.method: MVDGC uses sparse 3D cylindrical queries that model pedestrians as vertical cylinders with centers on the BEV plane and rectangular projections in image views. These queries serve as shape anchors that directly extract 2D features from intact image-view features via camera projection, avoiding distortion, and unify BEV and image-view localization into a single 3D cylinder position and shape refinement task.
AI.result: The paper claims MVDGC enables dual spatial constraints for precise localization, eliminates projection-induced distortions, and jointly optimizes BEV ground points and 2D bounding boxes. (Full quantitative results would be in the complete paper.)
AI.conclusion: By unifying BEV and image-view pedestrian localization via 3D cylindrical queries, MVDGC addresses key limitations of prior projection-based methods and provides a more geometrically consistent framework for multi-view pedestrian detection.

title: AEGIS: A Multi-Task Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for Mammography
summary: We present Aegis, a joint-embedding predictive architecture for breast cancer detection and density assessment in mammography. We train three Vision Transformer variants (Small/Base/Large) using self-supervised joint-embedding predictive architecture (JEPA) pre-training on 71,103 studies from 14 clinical sites, followed by supervised fine-tuning with progressive resolution scaling up to 2048x1536. On a curated 785-study test set, our largest model achieves area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) 0.949 for breast cancer triage with 93% sensitivity and 75% specificity at the optimal operating point. An ensemble combining our model with a U.S. Food and Drug Administration-cleared baseline further improves discrimination to 0.952 AUC. For breast density classification, the model achieves 0.953 AUC for binary (dense vs. non-dense) classification and 62.6% exact accuracy across four Breast Imaging Reporting and Data System (BI-RADS) categories, with 98.8% adjacent accuracy comparable to reported human inter-reader agreement. External validation on the public VinDr-Mammo dataset provides evidence of cross-population transfer under a different reference standard, with the largest model achieving 0.871 AUC for triage in a zero-shot setting.

AI.tldr: Aegis uses self-supervised JEPA pre-training with Vision Transformers on 71K+ mammography studies, achieving AUC 0.949 for breast cancer detection and 0.953 for density classification, with evidence of cross-population transfer.
AI.motivation: To improve breast cancer detection and density assessment in mammography using self-supervised learning that doesn't require extensive labeled data, while achieving high accuracy comparable to or exceeding existing methods.
AI.method: Trained Vision Transformer (Small/Base/Large) variants using self-supervised JEPA pre-training on 71,103 studies from 14 clinical sites, then fine-tuned with progressive resolution scaling up to 2048x1536.
AI.result: AUC 0.949 for cancer triage (93% sensitivity, 75% specificity); ensemble with FDA-cleared baseline achieved 0.952 AUC; 0.953 AUC for dense vs. non-dense classification; 62.6% exact accuracy across 4 BI-RADS categories; zero-shot AUC 0.871 on VinDr-Mammo.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised JEPA pre-training is effective for mammography analysis, achieving strong performance on both cancer detection and density classification with cross-population transfer capability.

title: OnPoint: Offline-to-Online Multi-Level Distillation for Point-Supervised Online Temporal Action Localization
summary: Temporal Action Localization (TAL) typically relies on segment annotations or offline access to full videos, limiting scalability and online use. We introduce Point-Supervised Online TAL (POTAL), which localizes actions in streaming videos using only one temporal point per instance. To solve POTAL, we propose OnPoint, an offline-to-online multi-level distillation framework that transfers knowledge from a point-supervised offline teacher to an online student via (i) pseudo-segment instance distillation, (ii) class-activation sequence distillation, and (iii) anticipatory window-level distillation. We further improve robustness by incorporating the original point labels into student training and by refining anchor decoding with actionness-guided attention calibration. Experiments on five datasets show OnPoint consistently outperforms strong baselines, establishing a solid foundation for POTAL.

AI.tldr: POTAL introduces point-supervised online temporal action localization using an offline-to-online distillation framework (OnPoint) that transfers knowledge through multi-level distillation, outperforming baselines across five datasets.
AI.motivation: TAL relies on segment annotations or offline full-video access, which limits scalability and real-time use. The authors aim to enable online TAL with minimal supervision (single point per instance).
AI.method: OnPoint uses an offline-to-online multi-level distillation framework with three components: (i) pseudo-segment instance distillation, (ii) class-activation sequence distillation, (iii) anticipatory window-level distillation. Point labels are incorporated into student training, and anchor decoding is refined via actionness-guided attention calibration.
AI.result: OnPoint consistently outperforms strong baselines on five datasets, establishing a solid foundation for point-supervised online TAL.
AI.conclusion: Point-supervised online TAL (POTAL) is feasible and effective; OnPoint provides a strong baseline for this new task, balancing minimal supervision with streaming video localization.

title: Rosetta: Composable Native Multimodal Pretraining
summary: Achieving true artificial general intelligence requires foundation models capable of integrating new modalities without forgetting prior knowledge. However, accommodating continuous generative objectives alongside discrete understanding tasks causes severe gradient conflicts. Existing architectures, including standard Mixture-of-Experts (MoE), are highly susceptible to representation overwriting. Even structurally partitioned paradigms like Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) remain vulnerable to catastrophic forgetting, severely impeding multimodal scalability. In this work, we introduce Rosetta, a composable native multimodal pretraining framework designed for seamless and non-destructive modality expansion. Rosetta adopts a modular paradigm where core foundational knowledge is preserved within global shared experts, while modality-specific capabilities are distributed across plug-and-play experts. To guarantee non-destructive composition, we propose Momentum-Anchored Orthogonal Projection (MAOP). MAOP leverages the optimizer's momentum state as an implicit semantic anchor, selectively neutralizing conflicting gradient components from new modalities while preserving synergistic updates. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that, while standard MoE and MoT architectures suffer catastrophic forgetting of previously acquired knowledge, Rosetta robustly preserves established language and visual understanding. Furthermore, it delivers superior image generation and unlocks cross-modal synergy, paving the way for truly composable and unified multimodal foundation models. To facilitate further multimodal research, we release our code and checkpoints to the community. Project page at https://rosetta-lmm.github.io/.

AI.tldr: Rosetta is a modular multimodal pretraining framework that preserves prior knowledge via shared experts and uses Momentum-Anchored Orthogonal Projection to prevent gradient conflicts, outperforming MoE and MoT in avoiding catastrophic forgetting while enabling cross-modal synergy.
AI.motivation: Existing architectures like MoE and MoT suffer from representation overwriting and catastrophic forgetting when integrating new modalities, impeding scalable multimodal learning.
AI.method: Rosetta uses a modular paradigm with global shared experts for foundational knowledge and plug-and-play experts for modality-specific capabilities, plus Momentum-Anchored Orthogonal Projection (MAOP) that leverages optimizer momentum as an implicit semantic anchor to neutralize conflicting gradients.
AI.result: While MoE and MoT suffer catastrophic forgetting, Rosetta preserves established language and visual understanding, delivers superior image generation, and unlocks cross-modal synergy.
AI.conclusion: Rosetta provides a non-destructive, composable path toward unified multimodal foundation models that can integrate new modalities without forgetting prior knowledge.

title: Learning When to Listen: Gated Affect Fusion for Human Motion Prediction
summary: Human motion forecasting in unconstrained real-world videos remains challenging due to the ambiguity of future behaviors and the presence of noisy multimodal observations. While facial affect potentially provides complementary behavioral cues, its practical utility and mechanistic boundaries within motion forecasting frameworks remain poorly understood. In this work, we present a systematic study investigating the utility and temporal limitations of affect-conditioned forecasting in-the-wild. We establish a rigorous multimodal pipeline combining MediaPipe body pose trajectories with HSEmotion facial affect representations, and introduce the Gated Affect Transformer (GAT) to dynamically regulate cross-modal information flow. Through extensive multi-horizon evaluations under a strict subject-wise protocol, we demonstrate that naive early cross-modal concatenation consistently degrades forecasting accuracy relative to pose-only baselines. Conversely, our proposed gating mechanism stabilizes cross-modal integration by adaptively controlling the affective stream. Crucially, controlled counterfactual experiments using shuffled and randomized affect inputs reveal that the learned gate successfully suppresses unstructured cross-modal noise while remaining responsive to plausible affective signals. Furthermore, our empirical results indicate that facial affect features provide bounded, horizon-dependent predictive cues strictly within short-to-medium windows (e.g., 30 frames), whereas long-term trajectories remain predominantly governed by intrinsic kinematic continuity. Our findings provide empirical evidence that facial affect should be regarded as a complementary behavioral cue rather than a dominant driver of future motion, offering practical guidance for selective multimodal fusion in unconstrained human motion forecasting.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether facial affect features improve human motion forecasting in real-world videos. The authors find that naive fusion of affect and pose data hurts performance, but their proposed Gated Affect Transformer (GAT) adaptively controls cross-modal information flow, leading to improvements only within short-to-medium time horizons (≤30 frames), while long-term predictions are dominated by kinematic continuity. Facial affect is thus a complementary, not dominant, cue.
AI.motivation: Human motion forecasting in unconstrained videos is challenging due to ambiguity and noisy multimodal observations. Facial affect may provide complementary behavioral cues, but its practical utility and temporal boundaries in motion forecasting are poorly understood.
AI.method: The authors build a multimodal pipeline combining MediaPipe body pose trajectories with HSEmotion facial affect representations, and introduce the Gated Affect Transformer (GAT) to dynamically regulate cross-modal information flow. They conduct multi-horizon evaluations under strict subject-wise protocols and perform counterfactual experiments with shuffled/randomized affect inputs.
AI.result: Naive early cross-modal concatenation degrades forecasting accuracy vs. pose-only baselines. The gating mechanism stabilizes cross-modal integration. Facial affect provides bounded, horizon-dependent predictive cues within short-to-medium windows (≤30 frames), while long-term trajectories are governed by kinematic continuity.
AI.conclusion: Facial affect should be regarded as a complementary behavioral cue, not a dominant driver of future motion. This offers practical guidance for selective multimodal fusion in unconstrained human motion forecasting.

title: Wake up for Touch! Mask-isolated Tactile Alignment Learning in MLLMs
summary: Touch supplies the physical grounding needed to perceive intrinsic material properties, such as friction and compliance, that vision alone often cannot resolve. Recent efforts for equipping multimodal LLMs with this tactile sense, however, expose a zero-sum trade-off: the limited parameter budget of compact models forces a choice between acquiring the new sensory modality and preserving the established vision-language reasoning. We present Splash, a mask-isolated tactile alignment learning framework for MLLMs. Splash quantifies the significance of each pretrained parameter, and partitions the parameter space into a dormant and critical subspace. While the frozen critical subspace acts as a stable anchor to safeguard general visual knowledge, Splash updates the isolated dormant subspace to internalize tactile alignment towards LLMs. This selective, non-destructive expansion effectively prevents catastrophic forgetting and ensures non-destructive modality expansion. Extensive experiments show that Splash effectively achieves tactile reasoning without additional inference overhead in the LLM part, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance on visuo-tactile benchmarks, including SSVTP, TVL, and TacQuad, while preserving its original general-purpose capabilities.

AI.tldr: Splash introduces a mask-isolated tactile alignment learning framework that partitions pretrained parameters into dormant and critical subspaces, updating only the dormant ones to add tactile reasoning to MLLMs without degrading original vision-language capabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing efforts to equip multimodal LLMs with tactile sensing suffer from a zero-sum trade-off: limited parameter budgets force a choice between acquiring tactile sense and preserving vision-language reasoning, leading to catastrophic forgetting.
AI.method: Splash quantifies parameter significance, partitions the parameter space into dormant and critical subspaces, freezes the critical subspace as a stable anchor, and selectively updates the dormant subspace to internalize tactile alignment without destructive interference.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on visuo-tactile benchmarks (SSVTP, TVL, TacQuad) while preserving original general-purpose capabilities, with no additional inference overhead in the LLM part.
AI.conclusion: Selective, non-destructive parameter expansion via subspace isolation effectively prevents catastrophic forgetting and enables successful tactile reasoning integration into MLLMs.

title: RetailSMV: Exocentric vs. Egocentric Adaptation of Foundation Video World Models in Retail
summary:
Foundation video diffusion models are increasingly viewed as world simulators for embodied agents, yet their pretraining on internet-scale generic video leaves them poorly aligned with real-world deployment domains. We study parameter-efficient adaptation of a pretrained foundation video world model to retail scenes: when synchronized egocentric and exocentric video of the same activity are available, which viewpoint of training data produces the strongest adapted model?
  We introduce RetailSMV (Retail Synchronized Multi-View), a corpus of 32,105 captioned retail clips from five supermarkets with synchronized ego/exo capture from the store-staff perspective (stocking, arranging, weighing, managing supply carts, scanning at checkout), rather than the customer-centric framing of prior retail video corpora, and train three matched Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) configurations of Cosmos3-Nano (egocentric-only, exocentric-only, combined) under identical hyperparameters. On a 200-clip held-out test set evaluated with seven complementary metrics under a strict paired statistical protocol, exocentric-only adaptation matches or exceeds combined adaptation on six of seven point estimates and is significantly better on LPIPS, PSNR, and DreamSim, despite training on only 15,985 exocentric clips (versus 32,105 for combined). A symmetric paired comparison further shows that adding exocentric data to egocentric-only training helps while adding egocentric data to exocentric-only training hurts. The absolute adaptation gap is largest at the shortest rollout time, identifying the near-horizon prediction window as the regime in which adaptation is most beneficial.

AI.tldr: Pretrained video diffusion models poorly align with retail domains. Using RetailSMV dataset (32K clips from 5 supermarkets with synchronized ego/exo views), exocentric-only LoRA adaptation outperforms egocentric and combined adaptation on most metrics, with near-horizon prediction benefiting most.
AI.motivation: Foundation video world models pretrained on generic internet video lack alignment with real-world deployment domains like retail. The authors ask which camera viewpoint (ego, exo, or combined) yields the best adapted model when synchronized multi-view data is available.
AI.method: Introduce RetailSMV corpus (32,105 captioned retail clips, 5 supermarkets, synchronized ego/exo from store-staff perspective). Train three LoRA configurations of Cosmos3-Nano: egocentric-only, exocentric-only, combined. Evaluate on 200-clip held-out test set with 7 complementary metrics under strict paired statistical protocol.
AI.result: Exocentric-only adaptation matches or beats combined on 6/7 metrics, significantly better on LPIPS, PSNR, DreamSim. Adding exo data helps egocentric training; adding ego data hurts exocentric training. Adaptation gap largest at shortest rollout time (near-horizon prediction benefits most).
AI.conclusion: For synchronized ego/exo retail video, exocentric-only adaptation is the most effective strategy. Near-horizon prediction window is where domain adaptation yields the greatest improvement.

title: Typography-Based Monocular Distance Estimation for Advanced Driver-Assistance Systems
summary: Estimating the distance to a leading vehicle is a basic input to forward collision warning, adaptive cruise control, and automated emergency braking. Production systems obtain this distance from radar, laser scanners, or stereo camera pairs, which add cost, power draw, and packaging constraints. This paper asks whether a single ordinary camera can recover the same distance by using a target that is standardized in size and present on every road vehicle: the rear license plate. U.S. plates share a fixed outer size and a character height that is set by regulation and varies only narrowly between states, so the height of a plate character in the image is a direct measure of distance once the camera geometry is known. The proposed method (Typography-Based Monocular Distance Estimation) detects the plate, measures the height of its printed characters, identifies the issuing state to select the correct physical character height, and recovers distance from the camera projection. Three measurements taken from the same plate: the character height, the stroke width, and the character spacing. Together with the spacing of the two mounting holes and a single-image depth network, are combined so that a weak or corrupted measurement is given less weight automatically. The distance, its rate of change, and a time-to-collision estimate are smoothed across frames and used to raise a warning with the timing used by U.S. collision-warning regulations. The same plate that anchors the scale also identifies the vehicle, so the method returns a distance, a bearing, and an identity from one passive sensor. It reads scale from a printed standard instead of from time of flight or parallax, making it a cheap, low-maintenance complement to those sensors in a fault-tolerant perception stack, achieving the cost-effective distance estimation with error less than 0.13 m.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a monocular distance estimation method (Typography-Based Monocular Distance Estimation) that uses a single ordinary camera to estimate distance to a leading vehicle by detecting the rear license plate, measuring the height of its printed characters, and using the standardized character dimensions to recover distance through camera projection geometry. The method achieves error less than 0.13 m.
AI.motivation: Production systems for forward collision warning, adaptive cruise control, and automated emergency braking rely on radar, laser scanners, or stereo camera pairs, which add cost, power draw, and packaging constraints. This paper asks whether a single ordinary camera can recover distance by using a standardized target present on every road vehicle: the rear license plate.
AI.method: The method (Typography-Based Monocular Distance Estimation) detects the license plate, measures the height of its printed characters, identifies the issuing state to select the correct physical character height, and recovers distance from the camera projection. Three measurements from the same plate (character height, stroke width, character spacing) plus mounting hole spacing and a single-image depth network are combined with automatic weighting to handle weak or corrupted measurements. Distance, rate of change, and time-to-collision are smoothed across frames.
AI.result: The method achieves cost-effective distance estimation with error less than 0.13 m.
AI.conclusion: The same plate that anchors scale also identifies the vehicle, so the method returns distance, bearing, and identity from one passive sensor. It reads scale from a printed standard instead of time of flight or parallax, making it a cheap, low-maintenance complement to existing sensors in a fault-tolerant perception stack.

title: CORGI: Consistency-Aware 3D Dog Reconstruction from a Single Image in the Wild
summary: Reconstructing high-fidelity 3D models of highly articulated animals, such as dogs, from a single in-the-wild image remains a formidable challenge. In this paper, we introduce CORGI, a novel framework for consistency-aware 3D dog reconstruction from a single unconstrained image that completely eliminates the need for 3D supervision. To overcome generative inconsistencies and the lack of multi-view capture, our pipeline introduces three core components. First, we propose a Canonical-Driven Orbital Generation (CDOG) strategy, utilizing specialized Canonical and Orbit LoRAs to normalize arbitrary input poses and synthesize reliable 360-degree video observations.
