Existing drawing-based 3D animation pipelines often use sample-wise 2D refinement to align animated renderings with the input image, but such optimization tends to overfit to the observed view and fails to correct projection-induced artifacts in novel views. To address this limitation, we introduce SPECSIA-15K, a paired stylization dataset containing 14,980 artifact-corrupted projection/refinement-target pairs from 1,498 3DBiCar characters. We further present DraViE (Drawing-based View Enhancement), a lightweight plug-and-play module trained with data-level priors to remove novel-view artifacts while preserving style and motion plausibility. Experiments show consistent gains in novel-view fidelity and temporal coherence with lower per-character adaptation cost than sample-wise fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: DraViE is a plug-and-play module trained on the SPECSIA-15K dataset to remove novel-view artifacts in single-drawing 3D animation while preserving style and coherence, outperforming per-sample fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing drawing-based 3D animation pipelines use per-sample 2D refinement, which overfits to the observed view and fails to correct projection-induced artifacts in novel views.
AI.method: The authors introduce SPECSIA-15K (14,980 artifact-corrupted projection/refinement-target pairs from 1,498 3DBiCar characters) and DraViE, a lightweight plug-and-play module trained with data-level priors to remove novel-view artifacts.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in novel-view fidelity and temporal coherence with lower per-character adaptation cost compared to sample-wise fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Data-level priors from a paired dataset can effectively replace per-sample optimization for artifact removal in drawing-based 3D animation.

title: NoPA: Non-Parametric Online 3D Scene Graph Generation
comment: This paper has been accepted in ECCV 26
summary: Classic 3D scene graph generation approaches fail to work in real-time due to the heavy computational cost of environment mapping and the need to generate intermediate point-cloud representations. To alleviate this issue, a recent work eschews point clouds in favor of a lightweight Gaussian distribution for each object. This approximation drastically speeds up inference and enables real-time 3D scene graph generation. However, the representation has two key weaknesses. \textbf{1)} Each object is approximated by a single 3D Gaussian, which causes a severe loss of 3D geometric detail. \textbf{2)} The discrepancy between this approximation and the true object geometry exacerbates the inaccurate merging of object candidates during online inference. To address these issues, we propose \textbf{NoPA}, which represents each object as a separate non-parametric distribution. This formulation retains 3D geometric information while preserving real-time inference of the parametric Gaussian formulation. To build upon our novel object representation, we propose a tailored merging strategy to recover coherent object instances. Specifically, we leverage maximum mean discrepancy on kernel density estimates to enable robust merging of object candidates during online exploration while minimizing added computational complexity. The key is to maintain a fixed particle set per object. Furthermore, to rectify the relation loss caused by misclassified objects, NoPA propagates relationships between objects with high affinity. Experiments show that NoPA substantially outperforms current methods without sacrificing real-time inference speed.

AI.tldr: NoPA proposes a non-parametric distribution representation for each object to replace single 3D Gaussian approximations, enabling real-time 3D scene graph generation with better geometric fidelity and more robust object merging via maximum mean discrepancy on kernel density estimates.
AI.motivation: Existing real-time 3D scene graph generation uses a single 3D Gaussian per object, which loses geometric detail and causes inaccurate merging of object candidates during online inference.
AI.method: NoPA represents each object as a non-parametric distribution with a fixed particle set, uses maximum mean discrepancy on kernel density estimates for robust merging, and propagates relationships between high-affinity objects.
AI.result: NoPA substantially outperforms current methods without sacrificing real-time inference speed.
AI.conclusion: Non-parametric object representation preserves geometric information while maintaining real-time performance, and the proposed merging strategy effectively recovers coherent object instances.

title: GEAR-Seg: A Grounded Explainable Agent for Reasoning Segmentation and Data Engine
summary: Reasoning segmentation requires localizing targets based on complex, implicit queries. Current end-to-end models typically entangle perception and deduction into an opaque black box, severely limiting interpretability and scalability. To address this, we propose GEAR-Seg (Grounded Explainable Agent for Reasoning Segmentation), an explicitly decoupled agent that shifts the paradigm by translating visual pixels into dense, attribute-rich text. By decoupling class-agnostic segmentation, semantic description, and Large Language Model (LLM) deduction, GEAR-Seg transforms implicit reasoning into an explicit, trackable logic chain. As a zero-shot inference framework, it achieves highly competitive performance across diverse reasoning and fine-grained referring segmentation benchmarks. Furthermore, GEAR-Seg inherently functions as a highly scalable data engine. Utilizing this engine, we construct GEAR-131K, a massive benchmark (over 38k images, 656k QA-mask pairs) introducing a multifaceted taxonomy tailored for complex real-world manipulation-oriented reasoning. Finally, distillation experiments demonstrate that lightweight models supervised exclusively by our automated pipeline closely match the upper-bound performance of costly human-annotated baselines.

AI.tldr: GEAR-Seg proposes an explainable, agent-based framework that decouples reasoning segmentation into explicit steps (segmentation → semantic description → LLM reasoning), achieving strong zero-shot performance and enabling automated data generation for scalable benchmark construction.
AI.motivation: Current end-to-end reasoning segmentation models entangle perception and deduction into an opaque black box, severely limiting interpretability and scalability.
AI.method: Decoupled agent framework with three explicit stages: (1) class-agnostic segmentation, (2) semantic description of visual pixels into dense attribute-rich text, (3) LLM-based deduction. Also serves as a data engine to construct GEAR-131K benchmark.
AI.result: Achieves highly competitive zero-shot performance across diverse reasoning and fine-grained referring segmentation benchmarks. Distillation experiments show lightweight models trained on automated pipeline match human-annotated baseline performance.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly decoupling perception and reasoning enhances interpretability without sacrificing performance, and the framework serves as a scalable data engine for constructing large-scale reasoning segmentation datasets.

title: ECoSim: Data Efficient Fine-Tuning for Controllable Traffic Simulation
summary: Controllable traffic simulation is critical for testing autonomous driving systems, yet existing approaches often require retraining large generative models with extensive annotated data. We introduce a lightweight control adaptation framework that enables multi-modal controllability (sketch, latent behavior codes, and text) for pretrained state-of-the-art diffusion and autoregressive traffic models. By modulating intermediate features through identity-initialized FiLM layers, our method efficiently adds new control modalities while preserving the base model's generative prior. Evaluated on Waymo Open Sim Agents Challenge, our approach demonstrates strong controllability with less than 1% of the paired control data. Through context-aware condition transfer, our framework enables counterfactual scenario generation and long-tail synthesis while maintaining stable closed-loop driving realism and safety. Our framework unlocks new possibilities for controllable traffic simulation, enabling targeted scenario generation through lightweight adaptation of pretrained generative models. Project page: https://ecosim-web.github.io/

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a lightweight control adaptation framework that adds multi-modal controllability (sketch, latent behavior codes, text) to pretrained diffusion and autoregressive traffic models using identity-initialized FiLM layers, achieving strong controllability with less than 1% of paired control data while preserving generative quality.
AI.motivation: Existing controllable traffic simulation approaches require retraining large generative models with extensive annotated data, which is inefficient and limits practical adoption.
AI.method: A lightweight control adaptation framework that modulates intermediate features of pretrained diffusion and autoregressive traffic models through identity-initialized FiLM layers, enabling multi-modal controllability via sketch, latent behavior codes, and text inputs.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong controllability with less than 1% of paired control data on the Waymo Open Sim Agents Challenge, maintaining stable closed-loop driving realism and safety.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables efficient, lightweight adaptation of pretrained generative models for controllable traffic simulation, unlocking new possibilities for counterfactual scenario generation and long-tail synthesis.

title: EgoGapBench: Benchmarking Egocentric Action Selection in Multi-Agent Scenes
comment: 15 pages, 2 figures, 8 tables. Code and benchmark are available at https://github.com/jhCOR/EgoGapBench
summary: Existing egocentric benchmarks have primarily constructed the egocentric setting from first-person-view data, which makes it difficult to evaluate egocentric perspective itself in isolation. However, understanding first-person-view input and taking an egocentric perspective are separable abilities, especially when first-person body cues are absent or when other agents are present. To isolate egocentric perspective understanding, we introduce EgoGapBench, a diagnostic benchmark for measuring action selection in multi-agent egocentric scenes. We define the ability measured by this benchmark as Egocentric Action Selection (EAS): selecting an appropriate action from the agent's perspective in the presence of other agents. On EgoGapBench, humans answer reliably, whereas both open-source and proprietary MLLMs perform substantially worse and systematically select actions performed by other visible agents. Fine-tuning on existing egocentric data fails to close this gap and can even be detrimental. In contrast, fine-tuning on EgoGapBench training data improves accuracy but does not reach human performance. These results show that EAS is difficult to acquire from first-person-view data alone, and that MLLMs should be evaluated and trained not only for scene understanding but also for egocentric action selection.

AI.tldr: EgoGapBench is a new benchmark that isolates egocentric perspective understanding from first-person-view perception, revealing that both open-source and proprietary MLLMs struggle with Egocentric Action Selection (EAS) — choosing appropriate actions from the agent's viewpoint when other agents are present. Humans perform reliably, but models systematically select actions of other visible agents. Fine-tuning on existing egocentric data fails to close this gap, while fine-tuning on EgoGapBench helps but doesn't reach human performance.
AI.motivation: Existing egocentric benchmarks conflate first-person-view perception with egocentric perspective understanding, making it impossible to evaluate true perspective-taking ability in isolation, especially when first-person body cues are absent or other agents are present.
AI.method: The authors introduce EgoGapBench, a diagnostic benchmark specifically designed to isolate egocentric perspective understanding by measuring Egocentric Action Selection (EAS): selecting appropriate actions from the agent's perspective in multi-agent scenes. They evaluate humans, open-source MLLMs, and proprietary MLLMs, and conduct fine-tuning experiments on existing egocentric data vs. EgoGapBench training data.
AI.result: Humans answer reliably on EgoGapBench, while MLLMs perform substantially worse and systematically select actions performed by other visible agents. Fine-tuning on existing egocentric data fails to close the gap and can even be detrimental. Fine-tuning on EgoGapBench improves accuracy but does not reach human performance.
AI.conclusion: EAS is difficult to acquire from first-person-view data alone. MLLMs need to be evaluated and trained not only for scene understanding but also specifically for egocentric action selection.

title: BrainFIBRE: A Foundation Model via Information Decomposition for Brain Microstructure
comment: ECCV 2026. The first three authors contributed equally
summary: Diffusion MRI probes brain microstructure with particular sensitivity to early cerebrovascular and neurodegenerative changes. Neurite Orientation Dispersion and Density Imaging (NODDI) decomposes the diffusion signal into three biophysically interpretable maps: neurite density index (NDI), orientation dispersion index (ODI), and free water fraction (FWF), capturing neurite packing, fiber coherence, and extracellular fluid. These 3D maps offer a rich substrate for transferable microstructural representations, yet integrating them is challenging: standard representation learning struggles to disentangle the unique information in each map from their shared and synergistic interactions. We present BrainFIBRE, the first foundation model for brain microstructure, pretrained on NODDI-derived maps from 55,592 UK Biobank participants. We propose Self-supervised Partial Information Decomposition (SPID), which extends PID-guided multimodal learning to the self-supervised regime for the first time. A novel Counterfactual Candidate Construction (CCC) paradigm perturbs inter-modality alignment through modality dropping and swapping, providing the contrastive signal for a Mixture-of-Experts architecture to disentangle unique, synergistic, and redundant information without any downstream label. On both Caucasian and Asian cohorts, BrainFIBRE achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse tasks predicting age, sex, cerebrovascular and neurodegenerative markers, and cognition, while yielding neurobiologically interpretable representations that reveal task- and cohort-specific interaction patterns. BrainFIBRE establishes a versatile foundation for neuroimaging analysis at the microstructural level.

AI.tldr: BrainFIBRE is the first foundation model for brain microstructure, pretrained on 55,592 UK Biobank NODDI maps using Self-supervised Partial Information Decomposition (SPID) to disentangle unique, synergistic, and redundant information across neurite density, orientation dispersion, and free water fraction maps, achieving state-of-the-art performance on diverse clinical and cognitive prediction tasks.
AI.motivation: Diffusion MRI's NODDI maps (NDI, ODI, FWF) provide rich microstructural information, but standard representation learning fails to disentangle the unique, shared, and synergistic interactions across these three maps, limiting transferable and interpretable representations.
AI.method: BrainFIBRE uses Self-supervised Partial Information Decomposition (SPID)—extending PID-guided multimodal learning to self-supervision—with a Counterfactual Candidate Construction (CCC) paradigm that perturbs inter-modality alignment via modality dropping and swapping, providing contrastive signals for a Mixture-of-Experts architecture to disentangle information without labels.
AI.result: On both Caucasian (UK Biobank) and Asian cohorts, BrainFIBRE achieves state-of-the-art performance across tasks predicting age, sex, cerebrovascular markers, neurodegenerative markers, and cognition, while producing neurobiologically interpretable representations that reveal task- and cohort-specific interaction patterns.
AI.conclusion: BrainFIBRE establishes a versatile, interpretable foundation model for microstructural neuroimaging analysis, demonstrating strong transferability across populations and tasks.

title: Caption Bottleneck Models
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) provide interpretability by routing predictions through a layer of human-understandable concepts. However, defining an optimal concept set for a specific dataset remains an open challenge. Existing approaches rely on expensive expert annotations or LLM-generated lists based solely on class names. Even "open-vocabulary" variants typically depend on static concept sets, which restrict discovery and introduce label bias. Furthermore, traditional CBMs often suffer from information leakage, where unmodeled visual features bypass the bottleneck and compromise the integrity of the explanations. To overcome these limitations, we propose Caption Bottleneck Models (CaBM), a framework that circumvents the need for predefined concept sets by replacing rigid concept layers with free-form natural language. By representing images via LMM-generated captions and training a classifier strictly on this text, CaBM ensures a leakage-free architecture by construction. Additionally, by analyzing the text classifier post-training, CaBM autonomously discovers high-quality, dataset-specific concepts. Our results across fine- and coarse-grained benchmarks demonstrate that CaBM achieves competitive accuracy while preserving interpretability without the constraints of external dictionaries or manual labeling.

AI.tldr: CaBM replaces predefined concept sets in Concept Bottleneck Models with free-form natural language captions from Large Multimodal Models, achieving leakage-free interpretability and automatic concept discovery while maintaining competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing CBMs require expensive expert annotations or static concept sets, suffer from information leakage where unmodeled visual features bypass the bottleneck, and restrict discovery due to label bias.
AI.method: CaBM uses LMM-generated captions to represent images, trains a classifier strictly on caption text (ensuring leakage-free architecture), and then analyzes the text classifier post-training to autonomously discover high-quality, dataset-specific concepts.
AI.result: Competitive accuracy across fine- and coarse-grained benchmarks while preserving interpretability without external dictionaries or manual labeling.
AI.conclusion: CaBM effectively circumvents the need for predefined concept sets and achieves leakage-free interpretability, offering a promising alternative to traditional CBMs.

title: EPO: Boosting 3D Foundation Models with Edge-based Pose Optimization
summary: We introduce \textbf{Edge-based Pose Optimization (EPO)}, a trackless geometric optimization framework specifically designed to boost the Structure-from-Motion reconstructions generated by 3D Foundation Models. These models achieve rapid inference by bypassing the time-consuming feature extraction and matching stages of traditional pipelines, where explicit correspondences between each 3D point and multiple images, referred to as tracks, are established. However, their geometric accuracy currently falls short of traditional pipelines. While this can be addressed in a post-processing step via Bundle Adjustment-like refinement, doing so requires extracting feature tracks, thus defeating the original speed advantage. Instead, our fully differentiable framework uses edge map alignment as a proxy for geometric optimization, avoiding feature extraction and track construction entirely. Through extensive evaluation across multiple datasets and tasks, we demonstrate that EPO matches or outperforms Bundle Adjustment-like methods while requiring significantly lower runtime and memory. Notably, its reduced memory footprint makes EPO suitable for consumer-grade hardware, where competing refinement methods cannot run.

AI.tldr: EPO is a fast, memory-efficient, differentiable framework that boosts 3D reconstruction accuracy by aligning edge maps, avoiding traditional feature extraction and track construction.
AI.motivation: 3D Foundation Models are fast but geometrically less accurate than traditional SfM pipelines; post-hoc Bundle Adjustment refinement requires feature tracks, negating speed advantages.
AI.method: A fully differentiable optimization framework that uses edge map alignment as a proxy for geometric refinement, eliminating the need for feature extraction and track construction.
AI.result: EPO matches or outperforms Bundle Adjustment-like methods with lower runtime and memory, and runs on consumer-grade hardware where competing methods fail.
AI.conclusion: Edge-based optimization is an effective, efficient alternative to track-based refinement for 3D Foundation Models.

title: Active Spatial Guidance: Eliminating Injected Positional Mechanisms in Vision Transformers
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) commonly rely on injected positional mechanisms to address self-attention's permutation invariance. Motivated by the spatial regularities of natural images, we ask whether spatial organization can be induced from data rather than explicitly injected. Under controlled, matched from-scratch training, we propose Active Spatial Guidance (Guidance), a training-only objective that disables positional injection and applies an auxiliary 2D coordinate-regression loss to the final-layer patch tokens. The guidance head is used only during training and removed for inference; the deployed model consists of a positional-injection-free ViT encoder and the task-specific prediction module. Using DINOv3 ViT backbones, Guidance consistently improves performance on ImageNet-100 classification, ADE20K semantic segmentation, and Hypersim monocular depth estimation, outperforming strong injected baselines such as learned absolute positional embeddings and rotary positional embeddings under identical training protocols. On ImageNet-100, broader comparisons against representative injected positional designs further support Guidance's effectiveness. Guidance also improves robustness under resolution transfer, and multi-resolution training further strengthens accuracy across input sizes. Overall, our results suggest that spatial inductive bias in ViTs need not be architecturally injected, but can be shaped through training-time supervision. The code used for training and evaluation is publicly available in https://github.com/cloudlc/asg.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Active Spatial Guidance (Guidance), a training-only objective that removes explicit positional injection from Vision Transformers and instead uses an auxiliary 2D coordinate-regression loss on final-layer patch tokens. The approach consistently outperforms injected positional embeddings (learned absolute PE, rotary PE) on ImageNet-100, ADE20K segmentation, and Hypersim depth estimation, and improves robustness under resolution transfer.
AI.motivation: Vision Transformers (ViTs) rely on injected positional mechanisms to address self-attention's permutation invariance. The authors ask whether spatial organization can be learned from data rather than explicitly injected, motivated by the spatial regularities of natural images.
AI.method: Active Spatial Guidance (Guidance) is a training-only auxiliary objective that disables all positional injection and applies a 2D coordinate-regression loss to the final-layer patch tokens. The guidance head is used only during training and removed during inference.
AI.result: Guidance consistently outperforms learned absolute PE and rotary PE on ImageNet-100 classification, ADE20K segmentation, and Hypersim depth estimation, and improves robustness under resolution transfer. Multi-resolution training further strengthens accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Spatial inductive bias in ViTs need not be architecturally injected but can be effectively shaped through training-time supervision alone.

title: GADA: Geometry-Aware Deformable Aggregation for Image-Based Gaussian Splatting
summary: Gaussian Splatting has achieved significant improvements by incorporating warping-based techniques. However, such methods suffer from pixel-level inaccuracies due to uncertain geometry. This uncertainty leads to spatial misalignments in the warped images, which disrupt residual learning used in warping-based methods and fundamentally limit the gains of correction, particularly on thin structures and high-frequency details. Driven by our insight that useful visual cues are not lost but locally preserved under slight displacement, we propose Geometry-Aware Deformable Aggregation (GADA). This method introduces an iterative refinement module with deformable offsets to actively correct spatial misalignments and recover these displaced cues. Furthermore, to address the limitations of standard pipelines where visibility checks (i.e., thresholding) often discard valid pixels and multi-view warped image fusion relies on naive mean aggregation, our module is coupled with an implicit confidence weighting mechanism that selectively suppresses unreliable evidence. Consequently, our approach outperforms prior warping-based Gaussian Splatting, preserving high-frequency quality while achieving 2.13 times faster FPS.

AI.tldr: Gaussian Splatting warping methods suffer from misalignment due to uncertain geometry. The authors propose Geometry-Aware Deformable Aggregation (GADA), which uses iterative refinement with deformable offsets and implicit confidence weighting to correct spatial misalignments and improve fusion, achieving better quality and 2.13x faster FPS.
AI.motivation: Warping-based Gaussian Splatting methods suffer from pixel-level inaccuracies caused by uncertain geometry, leading to spatial misalignments that disrupt residual learning and limit correction quality, especially on thin structures and high-frequency details.
AI.method: Proposes GADA: an iterative refinement module that uses deformable offsets to actively correct spatial misalignments, coupled with an implicit confidence weighting mechanism to selectively suppress unreliable evidence during multi-view warped image fusion.
AI.result: Outperforms prior warping-based Gaussian Splatting, preserves high-frequency quality, and achieves 2.13 times faster FPS.
AI.conclusion: GADA effectively addresses geometry-induced misalignment in warping-based Gaussian Splatting, enabling both better quality and faster inference.

title: Semantic-Guided Reading Order Reconstruction in Historical Armenian Newspapers with LLMs
comment: International Conference on Pattern Recognition, 2026, Lyon, France
summary: This paper addresses reading order reconstruction in historical Armenian newspapers, which combine complex layouts with limited language resources. We introduce a new annotated dataset of 66 pages and compare geometric heuristics, YOLO-based layout parsing, an end-to-end document model ECLAIR, and a hybrid method combining semantic zone detection with a generative LLM. Our hybrid method achieves the lowest error rates of all evaluated approaches, reducing ordering errors by up to 76% over the strongest geometric baseline, and remains robust in multi-page settings and under noisy OCR. Rather than targeting production the method is designed as a data bootstrapping strategy enabling rapid annotation in highly under-resourced scenarios. Alongside the dataset, we release a specialized Tesseract OCR model for historical Armenian print.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a dataset of 66 annotated pages from historical Armenian newspapers and compares several methods for reading order reconstruction. A hybrid approach combining semantic zone detection with a generative LLM achieves the best results, reducing ordering errors by 76% over geometric baselines. The method is designed for data bootstrapping in low-resource settings, and a specialized Tesseract OCR model for historical Armenian print is also released.
AI.motivation: To address reading order reconstruction in historical Armenian newspapers, which have complex layouts and limited language resources—a challenging under-resourced scenario.
AI.method: The authors compare geometric heuristics, YOLO-based layout parsing, the ECLAIR end-to-end document model, and a hybrid method combining semantic zone detection with a generative LLM. A new annotated dataset of 66 pages is introduced.
AI.result: The hybrid method achieves the lowest error rates, reducing ordering errors by up to 76% compared to the strongest geometric baseline. It remains robust in multi-page settings and under noisy OCR.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid approach is not intended for production but as a data bootstrapping strategy for rapid annotation in highly under-resourced scenarios. A specialized Tesseract OCR model for historical Armenian print is also released.

title: Retrieved Images as Visual Thought: Training-Free Multimodal In-Context Learning for the Open-vs-Closed Gap
comment: 12 pages, 6 figures. Includes appendix. Introduces the MAAC-Bench benchmark
summary: Recent work on Thinking with Images makes vision a dynamic part of reasoning, but does so through generation: the model invokes external tools, synthesizes code, or imagines new imagery, each at the cost of a tool protocol, brittle code, or an expensive training pipeline. A fourth route makes vision dynamic without generating anything, by retrieving labeled exemplar images and reasoning over them, yet it remains underexplored despite being train-free. We present ReVisIT, a train-free framework that realizes this retrieval-based route by treating each retrieved image-label pair as a unit of visual thought. ReVisIT combines structured class definitions, per-query multimodal retrieval of exemplars, and alternating user/assistant injection of those exemplars before joint multi-attribute decoding, and degrades gracefully to whichever components a task admits. On VL-ICL Bench Fast Open MiniImageNet, Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B with ReVisIT reaches 98.5% at 4-shot, statistically indistinguishable from the 72B LLaVA-OneVision SOTA (98.7%) on this near-saturated task at about 1/2.4 the parameters, while the same backbone without the scaffold sits at chance. The turns layer alone adds 26.1 points to GPT-4.1 on free-form concept induction (Bongard-OpenWorld), and the full stack yields a 4-6 point macro gain across three backbones on MAAC-Bench, a new license-clean 27-class, 5-attribute benchmark, significant by paired bootstrap on the curator-derived attributes. Component analysis shows that retrieval-plus-turns is the universal lever while structured definitions are need-adaptive, and that 83% of the retrieval gain comes from retrieval quality rather than from the presence of exemplars. MAAC-Bench is released with a rubric-grounded LLM verification protocol that replaces author spot-check on subjective attributes.

AI.tldr: ReVisIT is a train-free framework that improves vision-language reasoning by retrieving labeled exemplar images and reasoning over them, achieving near-SOTA performance with significantly fewer parameters and showing consistent gains across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing "thinking with images" approaches rely on costly generation (tools, code, or training). The authors argue that a retrieval-based route—using labeled exemplar images without any training—remains underexplored despite being simpler and more efficient.
AI.method: ReVisIT combines structured class definitions, per-query multimodal retrieval of exemplar image-label pairs, and alternating user/assistant injection of those exemplars before joint multi-attribute decoding. It degrades gracefully when components are unavailable.
AI.result: Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B with ReVisIT reaches 98.5% (4-shot) on VL-ICL Bench, near the 72B LLaVA-OneVision SOTA (98.7%) at ~1/2.4 parameters. Turns alone add 26.1 points to GPT-4.1. Full stack yields 4-6 point macro gain across backbones on MAAC-Bench. 83% of retrieval gain comes from quality, not presence of exemplars.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-based visual reasoning is a viable, train-free alternative to generation-heavy approaches. Retrieval-plus-turns is the universal performance lever; structured definitions are task-dependent. MAAC-Bench and its LLM verification protocol are released as resources.

title: Diffusion-Based Multi-Class Normality for OOD Detection: An Application to CDP Authentication
comment: IEEE International Conference on Advanced Visual And Signal-Based Systems, Aug 2026, Lecce, Italy
summary: Reconstruction-based generative models offer a natural framework for unsupervised out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, but multi-class normality modelling requires a single detector to capture multiple in-distribution manifolds and produce comparable anomaly scores across classes. We study this problem in copy detection pattern (CDP) authentication, where authentic and counterfeit samples are visually similar but differ in subtle printing-and-digitisation (P\&D) signatures. We propose a diffusion based multi-class normality framework in which a single class-conditional ControlNet is trained exclusively on authentic CDPs from multiple P\&D classes and detects counterfeits through reconstruction error under authentic-class conditioning. We further introduce dual template masking, which hides complementary regions of the input template and scores only withheld pixels, reducing reliance on visible binary structure. On the Indigo 1 x 1 Base dataset, the proposed method outperforms traditional and adapted generative baselines under multi-class authentic-versus-counterfeit evaluation, without using counterfeit samples for training or threshold calibration.

AI.tldr: Proposes a diffusion-based multi-class normality framework using class-conditional ControlNet and dual template masking for unsupervised OOD detection in CDP authentication, outperforming baselines on the Indigo dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing reconstruction-based generative models struggle with multi-class normality modelling for OOD detection, particularly in CDP authentication where authentic and counterfeit samples are visually similar but differ in subtle printing-and-digitisation signatures.
AI.method: A single class-conditional ControlNet trained exclusively on authentic CDPs from multiple P&D classes detects counterfeits via reconstruction error under authentic-class conditioning. Dual template masking hides complementary template regions and scores only withheld pixels to reduce reliance on visible binary structure.
AI.result: Outperforms traditional and adapted generative baselines on the Indigo 1 x 1 Base dataset under multi-class authentic-versus-counterfeit evaluation, without using counterfeit samples for training or threshold calibration.
AI.conclusion: The proposed diffusion-based framework effectively addresses multi-class normality modelling for OOD detection in CDP authentication, achieving superior performance without requiring counterfeit samples during training.

title: Identifying Latent Concepts and Structures for Generalized Category Discovery
comment: This paper has been accepted by ICML2026
summary: Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) aims to recognize known classes while autonomously discovering novel ones in open-world settings. However, current approaches primarily focus on designing clustering objectives, often overlooking a critical bottleneck: standard vision backbones yield high-rank, entangled token representations that are ill-suited for unsupervised discovery of latent concepts and structures. In this paper, we propose Compositional Primitive Fields (CPF-GCD), a novel representation learning framework that reshapes the feature space to make such latent structure identifiable by enforcing a low-rank compositional organization. Our core hypothesis is that all categories, whether known or novel, can be expressed as compositions and spatial arrangements of a finite set of learnable visual primitives that capture reusable concepts. CPF instantiates this geometric constraint via a spatial field mechanism. Inserted between the backbone and the head, it rewrites noisy patch tokens through low-rank primitive mixtures, effectively decomposing images into reusable atomic parts and their spatial layouts. By explicitly modeling the spatial distribution of primitives, CPF enables novel categories to emerge naturally as new activation patterns over a shared vocabulary. This shifts the focus of representation from merely partitioning global embeddings to constructing a structured and separable primitive field. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CPF serves as a generic, plug-and-play module that consistently boosts performance across diverse GCD baselines, validating that identifying and leveraging low-rank compositional structure is a crucial inductive bias for open-world recognition.

AI.tldr: CPF-GCD introduces Compositional Primitive Fields, a plug-and-play module that enforces low-rank compositional structure in feature representations to improve Generalized Category Discovery by decomposing images into reusable visual primitives and their spatial layouts.
AI.motivation: Standard vision backbones produce high-rank, entangled token representations that are poorly suited for unsupervised discovery of latent concepts and structures in open-world settings.
AI.method: CPF inserts a spatial field mechanism between backbone and head that rewrites noisy patch tokens into low-rank primitive mixtures, modeling images as compositions and spatial arrangements of a finite set of learnable visual primitives.
AI.result: CPF consistently boosts performance across diverse GCD baselines as a generic, plug-and-play module, demonstrating that low-rank compositional structure is a crucial inductive bias.
AI.conclusion: Identifying and leveraging low-rank compositional organization in feature space is key for open-world recognition; novel categories emerge naturally as new activation patterns over a shared primitive vocabulary.

title: Learning to Watch: Active Video Anomaly Understanding via Interleaved Policy Optimization
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026; 25 pages, 8 figures, 15 tables
summary: Video anomaly understanding (VAU) relies on sparse, context-dependent cues. However, existing passive paradigms suffer from observational aliasing, where static sampling fails to disambiguate semantically distinct events. To overcome this, we propose $Anom\text{-}π$, a closed-loop framework that reconceptualizes video understanding as an active sequential decision-making process within a dynamic environment. Inspired by human video-reviewing behavior, this framework unifies internal cognitive reasoning and strategic evidence acquisition into an interleaved policy, utilizing temporal atomic operators such as local backtracking, temporal expansion, and fine-grained sampling to endow the model with perceptual proactivity. To learn such complex interaction strategies under video-level weak supervision, we design Interactive Direct Preference Optimization (iDPO) to achieve trajectory-level policy alignment, guided by an Active Evidence Inquiry (AEI) utility that balances task success, informative evidence acquisition, and interaction cost. This approach enables the agent to learn to actively disambiguate hypotheses while suppressing redundant exploration. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework, with only 2B parameters, achieves highly competitive performance, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art large-scale VAU models in complex scenarios.

AI.tldr: Anom-π is a closed-loop active video anomaly understanding framework that treats video analysis as a sequential decision-making process, using temporal atomic operators and Interactive Direct Preference Optimization to achieve competitive performance with only 2B parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing passive VAU paradigms suffer from observational aliasing—static sampling fails to distinguish semantically distinct events, limiting understanding in complex, sparse, context-dependent scenarios.
AI.method: The framework unifies internal reasoning and evidence acquisition via an interleaved policy with temporal atomic operators (backtracking, temporal expansion, fine-grained sampling), and uses Interactive Direct Preference Optimization (iDPO) with Active Evidence Inquiry (AEI) utility for trajectory-level policy alignment under weak supervision.
AI.result: With only 2B parameters, the model achieves highly competitive performance, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art large-scale VAU models in complex scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Active sequential decision-making (closed-loop perception) is an effective paradigm for video anomaly understanding, overcoming observational aliasing with minimal parameter cost.

title: Uncertainty-aware tree height change regression
summary: Monitoring canopy height change is essential for understanding carbon sinks and forest dynamics. Remote sensing enables consistent, large-scale observations of such changes, increasingly integrated with deep learning architectures such as Geospatial Foundation Models (GFMs). However, existing methods and datasets frame the problem as binary change detection, which overlooks both the continuous nature of change, especially for vegetation, and the inherent uncertainty in labels. We present the Canopy Height Change (CHC) dataset, providing 3 $\mathrm{m}$ resolution continuous canopy height differences and associated spatially resolved uncertainties across 10598 $\mathrm{km}^2$ of northern and western Spain. The dataset is paired with a co-located time series of PlanetScope satellite imagery. Based on the dataset, we introduce the task of uncertainty-aware change regression, associated metrics and strategies for fine-tuning GFMs. Furthermore, we evaluate state-of-the-art GFMs and highlight promising directions and remaining challenges for advancing continuous canopy height change estimation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new dataset (CHC) for continuous canopy height change estimation with uncertainty, paired with satellite imagery, and evaluates Geospatial Foundation Models for this task.
AI.motivation: Existing methods treat change detection as binary, ignoring the continuous nature of vegetation change and label uncertainty. The authors aim to address these gaps.
AI.method: The authors created the CHC dataset (3m resolution, 10,598 km² in Spain) with continuous height differences and uncertainty estimates, plus PlanetScope imagery. They define an uncertainty-aware change regression task with metrics and fine-tuning strategies for GFMs.
AI.result: They evaluated state-of-the-art GFMs, identifying promising directions and remaining challenges for continuous canopy height change estimation.
AI.conclusion: Continuous change regression with uncertainty awareness is a more informative approach than binary change detection; current GFMs show potential but still face challenges.

title: Not All Prediction Targets Keep Training-Free Diffusion Guidance on the Manifold
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. 15-page main paper with appendix (48 pages total, 14 figures). Project page: https://manluml.github.io/on-manifold-tfg
summary: Training-free guidance (TFG) steers a pretrained diffusion model toward a desired attribute at inference. To be effective, this guidance must be applied from the earliest, high-noise steps of sampling. Because its objective (a classifier or energy) is defined on clean images, $ε$- and $v$-prediction models must first estimate the clean image $\hat{x}$ from the noisy state at each step, and the accuracy of that estimate determines how easily guidance drifts off the data manifold. $x$-prediction, a recent alternative, outputs the clean image directly, removing this source of error even at high noise. This is our motivation. We provide a theoretical analysis of how each prediction target shapes this accuracy, and introduce guided-class FID (Child FID), a metric that exposes the manifold damage standard evaluation misses. Experiments on a new fine-grained bird benchmark and on style transfer confirm that $x$-prediction keeps guided samples on the manifold most reliably, making it the strongest foundation for training-free guidance. Code is available at https://github.com/ManLuML/on-manifold-tfg

AI.tldr: The paper shows that x-prediction models enable more effective training-free guidance in diffusion models by producing more accurate clean-image estimates at high-noise steps, keeping samples on the data manifold better than ε- and v-prediction.
AI.motivation: Training-free guidance suffers from manifold drift during early high-noise sampling steps because ε- and v-prediction models must estimate clean images from noisy states, introducing errors. x-prediction outputs clean images directly, eliminating this error source.
AI.method: The authors theoretically analyze how different prediction targets (ε, v, x) affect clean-image estimation accuracy, introduce a new metric (Child FID) to measure manifold damage, and conduct experiments on fine-grained bird classification and style transfer tasks.
AI.result: x-prediction keeps guided samples on the data manifold most reliably across all tested tasks, outperforming ε- and v-prediction approaches.
AI.conclusion: x-prediction is the strongest foundation for training-free guidance in diffusion models due to its superior manifold preservation.

title: Linguistic Relative Policy Optimization for Video Anomaly Reasoning
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026; 18 pages, 8 figures, 9 tables
summary: Video anomaly detection (VAD) with multimodal large language models has shown strong potential, yet most existing methods still depend on large-scale annotations or expert-designed priors, limiting their ability to acquire anomaly knowledge with as little human intervention as possible. To address this, we propose Linguistic Relative Policy Optimization (LRPO), which distills group-relative semantic advantages from multiple reasoning trajectories into a linguistically expressed anomaly experience prior, and adapts the model by injecting this prior into the context to steer its output distribution without any parameter updates. LRPO builds two complementary experience representations: general experience captures transferable anomaly preferences across scenarios, while scenario experience models context-dependent anomaly rules for targeted refinement. To further improve the learned experience, we introduce an anomaly alignment reward that guides trajectory optimization to match human risk preferences and reinforce temporally grounded reasoning. Extensive experiments on XD-Violence, UCF-Crime, and UBnormal demonstrate that LRPO significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods under tuning-free settings.

AI.tldr: LRPO is a tuning-free method for video anomaly detection that distills anomaly knowledge from multiple reasoning trajectories into linguistic priors, using two complementary experience representations (general and scenario) and an anomaly alignment reward, outperforming SOTA on XD-Violence, UCF-Crime, and UBnormal.
AI.motivation: Existing VAD methods with multimodal LLMs still require large-scale annotations or expert-designed priors, limiting the ability to acquire anomaly knowledge with minimal human intervention. LRPO aims to reduce this dependency.
AI.method: LRPO distills group-relative semantic advantages from multiple reasoning trajectories into a linguistically expressed anomaly experience prior, injects this prior into context (no parameter updates), builds general + scenario experience representations, and uses an anomaly alignment reward to guide trajectory optimization.
AI.result: LRPO significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods under tuning-free settings on XD-Violence, UCF-Crime, and UBnormal datasets.
AI.conclusion: LRPO enables effective video anomaly detection with minimal human intervention by leveraging linguistically expressed experience priors and alignment rewards, achieving SOTA results without parameter updates.

title: DART: Difficulty-Adaptive Routing for Zero-Shot Video Temporal Grounding
comment: Accepted to the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV) 2026
summary: Zero-shot video temporal grounding (VTG) localizes events in untrimmed videos from natural language queries without task-specific training. Existing methods rely on frame-query feature matching, which suffices for simple events but struggles with complex multi-stage queries that require understanding temporal ordering and causal structure -- a disparity we call the reasoning gap. We propose DART (Difficulty-Adaptive Routing for Temporal Grounding), which bridges this gap by coupling difficulty-aware routing with structured reasoning in large vision-language models. A query-conditioned Determinantal Point Process (DPP) serves a dual role: selecting diverse, query-relevant keyframes as temporal evidence, and providing spectral entropy as a difficulty indicator. Simple queries are routed to a Fast path for direct prediction, while complex queries follow a Slow path with Temporal Markup Prompting, which decomposes localization into global event analysis, per-frame temporal role annotation, and boundary extraction. On Charades-STA and ActivityNet Captions, DART achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance across both identically distributed and multiple out-of-distribution settings, improving mIoU by up to 3.5 points over the strongest baseline while using over 7 times fewer frames. The project homepage is available at https://dart-vtg.github.io/.

AI.tldr: DART bridges the reasoning gap in zero-shot video temporal grounding by using difficulty-aware routing: simple queries use direct frame-query matching, while complex multi-stage queries are routed through structured reasoning with Temporal Markup Prompting, guided by a DPP-based difficulty indicator.
AI.motivation: Existing zero-shot VTG methods fail on complex multi-stage queries requiring temporal ordering and causal understanding, creating a "reasoning gap" that needs bridging.
AI.method: DART uses a query-conditioned DPP for keyframe selection and difficulty estimation (spectral entropy). Simple queries follow a Fast path (direct prediction); complex queries follow a Slow path using Temporal Markup Prompting (global analysis, per-frame annotation, boundary extraction).
AI.result: SOTA zero-shot performance on Charades-STA and ActivityNet Captions in both ID and OOD settings, with up to 3.5 mIoU improvement over baselines while using 7x fewer frames.
AI.conclusion: Difficulty-adaptive routing effectively closes the reasoning gap in zero-shot VTG, achieving high efficiency and accuracy by tailoring inference strategy to query complexity.

title: ABot-M0.5: Unified Mobility-and-Manipulation World Action Model
summary: Mobile manipulation is a key capability for general-purpose robots, yet remains challenging for current embodied learning methods. VLA policies are typically reactive and lack explicit world modeling, while existing World Action Models (WAMs) are still poorly aligned with the structure of mobile manipulation: they operate on coarse video chunks, model entangled navigation-manipulation actions, and train inverse dynamics under supervision that does not match autoregressive inference. As a result, they often miss fine-grained contact dynamics, suffer from action-distribution conflicts, and accumulate errors over long-horizon rollouts. We propose ABot-M0.5, a new WAM built on the insight that mobile manipulation requires alignment at three levels: temporal granularity, action space, and train-test consistency. To align temporal granularity, we introduce intermediate latent actions that capture local visual state transitions and serve as an bridging action space between video latents and embodiment-specific controls. To align action space, we design a dual-level Mixture-of-Transformers architecture that disentangles both modality representations and heterogeneous action subspaces such as base movement and arm manipulation. To align inference conditions, we propose the dream-forcing training strategy that progressively trains inverse dynamics on model-predicted videos, improving train-test alignment and robustness during autoregressive prediction. Experiments on challenging mobile and fine-grained manipulation benchmarks demonstrate that ABot-M0.5 achieves state-of-the-art performance in both long-horizon task success and finegrained control accuracy. These results highlight the critical importance of granularity-aligned, action-disentangled, and inference-consistent world-action modeling.

AI.tldr: ABot-M0.5 proposes a World Action Model for mobile manipulation with three alignment improvements: temporal granularity via intermediate latent actions, action space disentanglement via dual-level Mixture-of-Transformers, and train-test consistency via dream-forcing training, achieving SOTA results on long-horizon and fine-grained tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA policies and World Action Models (WAMs) are poorly aligned with mobile manipulation's structure — they use coarse video chunks, entangled navigation-manipulation actions, and mismatched training vs. inference conditions, causing missed contact dynamics, action conflicts, and error accumulation over long horizons.
AI.method: Three alignment strategies: (1) Temporal alignment via intermediate latent actions bridging video latents and embodiment controls; (2) Action space alignment via dual-level Mixture-of-Transformers disentangling modality representations and heterogeneous action subspaces (base movement vs. arm manipulation); (3) Train-test alignment via dream-forcing training that progressively trains inverse dynamics on model-predicted videos.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on challenging mobile and fine-grained manipulation benchmarks, excelling in both long-horizon task success and fine-grained control accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Granularity-aligned, action-disentangled, and inference-consistent world-action modeling is critically important for effective mobile manipulation, and ABot-M0.5 demonstrates the effectiveness of addressing these three alignment levels.

title: LUMA: Benchmarking Segmentation via a Lightweight Universal Mask Adapter
summary: Comparing transformer backbones for image segmentation is confounded: each is paired with a different decoder, recipe, and pretraining, so reported differences rarely reflect the backbone itself. We introduce the Lightweight Universal Mask Adapter (LUMA), a lightweight, backbone-agnostic mask-transformer head that treats any backbone as a black-box feature extractor, letting a set of queries read from its features through cheap cross-attention. LUMA matches the accuracy of EoMT, the state-of-the-art efficient ViT-segmenter, at lower cost, while attaching unchanged to isotropic, hierarchical, convolutional, and mixture-of-experts backbones alike. Holding this head fixed, we benchmark 20 backbones, 11 pretraining schemes and a range of resolutions on ADE20K and Cityscapes under one modern recipe. We find that ``efficient'' token mixers fail to deliver efficiency even at the high resolutions that motivate them, with plain ViT holding the throughput Pareto-front at every resolution. Additionally, the pretraining objective, not the architecture, the lever the field has tuned hardest, governs segmentation quality.

AI.tldr: LUMA is a lightweight, backbone-agnostic mask-transformer head that enables fair benchmarking of 20 backbones and 11 pretraining schemes for image segmentation, revealing that plain ViT dominates efficiency Pareto-front and pretraining objective matters more than architecture for segmentation quality.
AI.motivation: Comparing transformer backbones for image segmentation is confounded because each backbone is paired with different decoders, recipes, and pretraining, making it impossible to isolate the backbone's true contribution.
AI.method: Introduces LUMA, a lightweight, backbone-agnostic mask-transformer head that treats any backbone as a black-box feature extractor using cheap cross-attention from learnable queries.
AI.result: 1) LUMA matches EoMT accuracy at lower cost across diverse backbones. 2) "Efficient" token mixers fail to deliver efficiency at high resolutions; plain ViT holds throughput Pareto-front. 3) Pretraining objective, not architecture, governs segmentation quality.
AI.conclusion: The field's most tuned lever (pretraining objective) is the primary driver of segmentation quality, and plain ViT remains the most efficient backbone across resolutions.

title: Imprint: Online Memory Compression for Long-Horizon Egocentric QA
summary: Long-horizon egocentric question answering involves answering about events that have occurred hours or days in the past. This requires memory representations that remain both retrieval-effective and scalable over days or weeks of recording. Existing long-horizon egocentric QA methods construct memory as hierarchical textual summaries of observations. While effective for reducing memory size, summarization optimizes for descriptive compression rather than retrieval: repeated interactions are absorbed into coarse textual descriptions instead of being preserved as explicit, recurring memory units, making long-horizon evidence aggregation difficult. We propose Imprint, an interaction-centric memory framework that formulates long-horizon egocentric memory as an online memory compression problem rather than summarization. Incoming observations are first represented as structured Interaction Records and continuously organized into recurring interaction patterns. Using human memory consolidation signals of recurrence, recency, and distinctiveness, Imprint selectively retains and compresses interactions into a compact retrieval-oriented memory. We evaluate Imprint on EgoLifeQA, a seven-day egocentric benchmark containing questions that require reasoning over interactions occurring hours to days before the query. With the same LLM, Imprint improves QA accuracy from 31.0% to 35.8%, increases evidence-grounded answers by $6\times$ compared with EgoRAG, reduces memory footprint by $2.3\times$, and decreases retrieval latency by $11.8\times$. These results demonstrate that memory compression provides a scalable and retrieval-effective foundation for long-horizon egocentric question answering.

AI.tldr: Imprint proposes an interaction-centric memory framework for long-horizon egocentric QA that treats memory as an online compression problem rather than summarization, using recurrence, recency, and distinctiveness signals to selectively retain interactions, achieving significant improvements in QA accuracy, evidence grounding, memory footprint, and retrieval latency.
AI.motivation: Existing long-horizon egocentric QA methods use hierarchical textual summaries, which optimize for descriptive compression rather than retrieval — repeated interactions get absorbed into coarse text instead of being preserved as explicit, recurring memory units, making long-horizon evidence aggregation difficult.
AI.method: Imprint formulates long-horizon egocentric memory as an online memory compression problem. Incoming observations are first represented as structured Interaction Records and continuously organized into recurring interaction patterns. Using human memory consolidation signals (recurrence, recency, distinctiveness), it selectively retains and compresses interactions into a compact retrieval-oriented memory.
AI.result: On EgoLifeQA benchmark, Imprint improves QA accuracy from 31.0% to 35.8%, increases evidence-grounded answers by 6× compared to EgoRAG, reduces memory footprint by 2.3×, and decreases retrieval latency by 11.8×.
AI.conclusion: Memory compression (interaction-centric, retrieval-oriented) provides a scalable and retrieval-effective foundation for long-horizon egocentric question answering, outperforming summarization-based approaches.

title: Creating Impactful Autonomous Driving Datasets: A Strategic Guide from Research Gap to Benchmark
comment: Keywords: Autonomous Driving, Dataset Design, Benchmarks, Research Gap Identification. 14 pages, 3 figures
summary: Well-designed autonomous driving datasets have fundamentally shaped research progress, yet existing literature primarily describes what datasets contain rather than how to strategically design impactful ones. This is especially limiting for small and medium-sized labs and startups that cannot afford to misallocate scarce resources. We argue that impactful dataset creation begins with a diagnosis: whether a research question is blocked by a data problem or an evaluation problem, and proceeds by selecting the minimal data operator(s) that closes the resulting gap, recording new data only when no cheaper operator(s) suffices. We analyze the evolution of major autonomous driving (AD) datasets through this lens and distill a strategic framework spanning gap identification, operator choice, sensor suite design, and annotation strategy. We ground the framework in a running case study of our KITScenes dataset family. The datasets are available at: https://kitscenes.com/

AI.tldr: The paper presents a strategic framework for creating impactful autonomous driving datasets, emphasizing diagnosis of data vs. evaluation problems, minimal data operations, and resource efficiency—demonstrated through the KITScenes dataset family.
AI.motivation: Existing AD dataset literature focuses on what datasets contain, not how to strategically design them. Small/medium labs and startups need guidance to avoid misallocating scarce resources.
AI.method: Authors propose a diagnostic framework: identify if a research question is blocked by a data or evaluation problem, then select the minimal data operator(s) to close the gap, recording new data only when cheaper alternatives fail. They analyze major AD dataset evolution through this lens.
AI.result: A strategic framework covering gap identification, operator choice, sensor suite design, and annotation strategy, grounded in a KITScenes dataset case study.
AI.conclusion: Impactful dataset creation should follow a resource-aware, diagnosis-driven process rather than ad-hoc data collection. The framework helps smaller entities design effective datasets efficiently.

title: Towards Memory-Efficient Autoregressive Video Generation via Instance-Specific Parametric Absorption
summary: Autoregressive (AR) streaming models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for long video generation. However, the linearly growing Key-Value (KV) cache poses a significant bottleneck, leading to memory overload and degraded inference throughput. A common compression method is to drop redundant KV tokens, which often breaks long-range dependencies, resulting in temporal flickering and identity loss. In this paper, we propose Instance-Specific Parametric Absorption (ISPA), a novel framework that shifts the KV cache compression from discarding to distilling. The core idea is to transit a subset of layers from Full-Attention (F-Layers) to memory-efficient Local-Attention (L-Layers) by "absorbing" historical context into the model's weights. Specifically, during a brief warmup phase, ISPA monitors the output discrepancy between global and local attention. At the transition point, we solve a closed-form least-squares problem to compute an instance-specific weight modulation that compensates for the missing history. Experiments across architectures (1.3B to 14B) demonstrate that ISPA can remove up to 50\% of the KV cache with near-lossless visual quality. We hope this perspective encourages future work to explore parametric memory consolidation beyond external token-level cache management for streaming generative models.

AI.tldr: ISPA proposes a novel KV cache compression method for autoregressive streaming video models by shifting from discarding tokens to distilling historical context into model weights via instance-specific parametric absorption, achieving up to 50% KV cache reduction with near-lossless quality.
AI.motivation: The linearly growing Key-Value (KV) cache in autoregressive streaming models causes memory overload and degraded inference throughput. Existing compression methods that drop redundant KV tokens often break long-range dependencies, leading to temporal flickering and identity loss.
AI.method: ISPA transitions a subset of layers from Full-Attention (F-Layers) to memory-efficient Local-Attention (L-Layers) by absorbing historical context into model weights. During a warmup phase, it monitors output discrepancy between global and local attention, then at the transition point solves a closed-form least-squares problem for instance-specific weight modulation to compensate for missing history.
AI.result: Experiments across architectures from 1.3B to 14B parameters show ISPA can remove up to 50% of the KV cache with near-lossless visual quality.
AI.conclusion: ISPA encourages future work to explore parametric memory consolidation beyond external token-level cache management for streaming generative models.

title: Partial Skeleton Visibility for Action Recognition: A Constrained Field-of-View Approach
summary: Skeleton-based action recognition has achieved remarkable success by exploiting joint coordinates and their topological connections, yet prevailing methods overwhelmingly assume complete and clean skeleton inputs. In real-world deployments, such as egocentric vision, crowded surveillance, wearable devices, or edge robotics, limited field-of-view (FoV) frequently causes substantial joint visibility dropout, leading to severe performance degradation that existing models are largely unprepared to handle. To bridge this critical yet underexplored gap, we introduce PartialVisGraph, a novel hypergraph framework tailored for robust skeleton action recognition under constrained FoV. We first construct highly expressive hypergraphs by introducing learnable virtual hyperedges that form a soft incidence matrix, capturing flexible high-order dependencies beyond conventional pairwise graphs. We then propose the Single-Head Sample-Adaptive Transformer, which adaptively aggregates joint features onto hyperedges while explicitly incorporating a visibility prior. This prior selectively gates information flow, preventing occluded or out-of-view joints from corrupting reliable feature propagation. We further establish rigorous evaluation protocols with realistic FoV simulation benchmarks on NTU RGB+D 60 and 120. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PartialVisGraph consistently achieves state-of-the-art accuracy under partial visibility, with gains of up to 68.8\% on subsets with severe FoV restrictions compared to recent strong baselines, while remaining superior on full-visibility settings. Our approach offers a principled and practical pathway toward deployable skeleton-based action understanding in unconstrained environments.

AI.tldr: PartialVisGraph is a hypergraph framework with learnable virtual hyperedges and a Single-Head Sample-Adaptive Transformer that uses visibility priors to achieve robust skeleton-based action recognition under constrained field-of-view, outperforming baselines by up to 68.8% on severe FoV settings while maintaining full-visibility performance.
AI.motivation: Existing skeleton-based action recognition methods assume complete, clean inputs and fail under real-world partial visibility caused by limited field-of-view in egocentric vision, surveillance, wearables, and edge robotics. This critical gap remains underexplored.
AI.method: (1) Constructs expressive hypergraphs with learnable virtual hyperedges forming a soft incidence matrix for flexible high-order dependencies. (2) Proposes Single-Head Sample-Adaptive Transformer to adaptively aggregate joint features onto hyperedges with a visibility prior that gates information flow to prevent occluded joints from corrupting propagation.
AI.result: State-of-the-art accuracy under partial visibility on NTU RGB+D 60 and 120; gains up to 68.8% on subsets with severe FoV restrictions compared to strong baselines; remains superior on full-visibility settings.
AI.conclusion: PartialVisGraph offers a principled and practical pathway toward deployable skeleton-based action understanding in unconstrained, real-world environments with constrained field-of-view.

title: AV-SyncBench: Decoupled Benchmarking of Temporal and Semantic Audio-Visual Synchronization
summary: Audio-visual feature extraction is a fundamental component of multimodal understanding and generation tasks. However, existing evaluation protocols for feature extraction models exhibit dimensional bias, typically focusing on either semantic matching or temporal offset detection. Moreover, their data construction remains coupled, preventing independent assessment of temporal and semantic consistency. We propose AV-SyncBench, the first benchmark to fully separate temporal and semantic evaluation for audio-visual synchronization. Built from in-the-wild videos, it spans Voice, Music, and Sound across 10 scenarios and 5 challenge tasks. Data are automatically filtered and manually verified to ensure on-screen sound sources. The benchmark contains 3,269 videos and 38,390 samples, and we evaluate five representative models to quantify feature quality for alignment and downstream tasks. The code and dataset are available at: https://fgt7t6g.github.io/AV-SyncBench.

AI.tldr: AV-SyncBench is the first benchmark that fully separates temporal and semantic evaluation for audio-visual synchronization, containing 3,269 videos and 38,390 samples across Voice, Music, and Sound scenarios, with five models evaluated.
AI.motivation: Existing audio-visual feature extraction evaluation protocols suffer from dimensional bias (focusing on either semantic matching or temporal offset detection) and coupled data construction, preventing independent assessment of temporal and semantic consistency.
AI.method: The authors construct AV-SyncBench from in-the-wild videos spanning Voice, Music, and Sound across 10 scenarios and 5 challenge tasks. Data are automatically filtered and manually verified to ensure on-screen sound sources. The benchmark includes 3,269 videos and 38,390 samples.
AI.result: Five representative models are evaluated to quantify feature quality for alignment and downstream tasks. The benchmark separates temporal and semantic evaluation for audio-visual synchronization.
AI.conclusion: AV-SyncBench addresses the dimensional bias in existing evaluation protocols by providing a fully separated temporal and semantic evaluation benchmark for audio-visual synchronization, enabling more precise assessment of feature extraction models.

title: ConRTF: Edge-Constrained Boundary Distribution Refinement for Realtime TransFormer Table Structure Recognition
summary: Table Structure Recognition (TSR) aims to recover the row and column layout of tables from document images, a key step in document understanding pipelines. Accurate TSR depends on precise boundary localization: small errors in row or column boundaries can propagate into incorrect cell assignments and structural inconsistencies. Yet detection-based approaches treat table elements as generic objects, ignoring a fundamental property of table layout: rows and columns play structurally distinct roles and their boundaries carry unequal importance. We propose an Edge-constrained Fine-grained Localization loss (EFL) that formalizes this structural asymmetry by encoding table-specific geometric priors into the training objective: row-like elements are supervised with emphasis on their horizontal boundaries, while column-like elements prioritize vertical boundaries. Implemented within a real-time detector with distribution-based boundary refinement (D-FINE), EFL operates during training only and guides boundary refinement toward structurally meaningful adjustments with no change to the inference pipeline. The proposed approach, ConRTF, is also data-efficient, maintaining robust accuracy with as few as 2k--3k annotated tables. Experiments on PubTables-1M and two private datasets show consistent improvements over the optimized baseline and several real-time detectors including RT-DETRv2 and YOLOv10-11, with gains of up to +1.6 GriTS points at equal inference speed.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ConRTF, an edge-constrained fine-grained localization loss (EFL) for table structure recognition that leverages the structural asymmetry between rows and columns by emphasizing horizontal boundaries for rows and vertical boundaries for columns, achieving consistent improvements (up to +1.6 GriTS) over real-time detectors like RT-DETRv2 and YOLOv10-11, while maintaining data efficiency with as few as 2k-3k annotated tables.
AI.motivation: Existing detection-based TSR methods treat table elements as generic objects, ignoring that rows and columns have structurally distinct roles and their boundaries carry unequal importance. Small errors in boundary localization can propagate into incorrect cell assignments and structural inconsistencies.
AI.method: Proposes an Edge-constrained Fine-grained Localization loss (EFL) that encodes table-specific geometric priors: row-like elements are supervised with emphasis on horizontal boundaries, column-like elements prioritize vertical boundaries. EFL is implemented within D-FINE, a real-time detector with distribution-based boundary refinement, and operates during training only without changing inference.
AI.result: Evaluated on PubTables-1M and two private datasets, ConRTF achieves consistent improvements (+1.6 GriTS points max) over optimized baselines including RT-DETRv2 and YOLOv10-11, at equal inference speed. It is also data-efficient, maintaining accuracy with only 2k-3k annotated tables.
AI.conclusion: ConRTF effectively leverages table structural asymmetry through a training-only loss to improve boundary localization for TSR, outperforming state-of-the-art real-time detectors while preserving inference efficiency.

title: Towards Robust Driving Perception: A Flexible Scale-Driven Family for Self-Supervised Monocular Depth Estimation
comment: Accepted by ECCV2026. Code is available at https://github.com/startnew/flexdepth
summary: Self-Supervised Monocular Depth Estimation (MDE) has garnered attention in recent years due to its independence from ground truth. However, most existing models are limited to a single scale and exhibit considerable performance degradation in complex driving environments. Networks specifically designed to handle dynamic traffic participants tend to be overly complex, hindering their deployment on resource-constrained automotive edge devices. To address these limitations and move towards robust driving perception, we propose FlexDepth, a scale-driven and flexible family of self-supervised MDE models tailored for challenging road scenarios. FlexDepth employs a two-stage static-dynamic decoupled training strategy, enabling the independent assessment of confidence for both static backgrounds and dynamic road objects. Furthermore, it introduces a meticulously designed Scale-Driven Decoder (SDD) to dynamically select components based on scale size, facilitating efficient feature fusion and the output of high-precision depth maps. Extensive experiments on standard driving benchmarks demonstrate that without any auxiliary information, our model achieves state-of-the-art performance across arbitrary scales with minimal computational overhead. Our smallest model, Flex-Nano, requires only 0.7 GFLOPs and achieves 37.6 FPS on mobile platforms, ensuring reliable real-time perception while maintaining excellent zero-shot generalization.Our source code is avalible: https://github.com/startnew/flexdepth

AI.tldr: FlexDepth is a scalable family of self-supervised monocular depth estimation models for driving scenes, using a static-dynamic decoupled training strategy and a Scale-Driven Decoder to achieve state-of-the-art performance across scales with minimal compute (e.g., Flex-Nano: 0.7 GFLOPs, 37.6 FPS).
AI.motivation: Existing self-supervised MDE models are limited to single scale and degrade in complex driving scenes; dynamic-aware networks are too complex for edge deployment.
AI.method: Two-stage static-dynamic decoupled training strategy for independent confidence assessment of backgrounds and dynamic objects, plus a Scale-Driven Decoder (SDD) that dynamically selects components based on scale for efficient feature fusion.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance across arbitrary scales on standard driving benchmarks; Flex-Nano achieves 0.7 GFLOPs and 37.6 FPS on mobile platforms with strong zero-shot generalization.
AI.conclusion: FlexDepth enables robust, real-time depth perception on resource-constrained automotive edge devices without auxiliary information or ground truth.

title: Prototype Memory-Guided Training-Free Anomaly Classification and Localization in Prenatal Ultrasound
summary: Prenatal anomaly classification and localization is of critical importance for fetal health and pregnancy management. Although ultrasound (US) is the primary modality for prenatal screening, accurate diagnosis remains challenging due to the low prevalence and high heterogeneity of anomalies. Existing deep learning methods for prenatal tasks rely on large-scale annotated datasets, which are difficult to obtain in practice. Although few-shot learning alleviates data scarcity, it typically requires fine-tuning for new categories, limiting its practicality in resource-limited clinical settings. To address these challenges, we propose a training-free framework for multi-class prenatal US anomaly classification and localization that operates with only a few reference images per class, representing the first exploration of this setting. Our framework comprises three key components: (1) a memory bank with multi-granular prototypes that explicitly models both class-level semantics and anomaly characteristics; (2) a prototype-driven soft merging mechanism that aggregates discriminative features to detect the anomaly region; and (3) a class-aware refinement strategy that leverages prototype consistency to improve category prediction. Extensively validated on a multi-center prenatal US dataset containing 1,149 cases, with a total of 2,357 images and 9 categories, our proposed method outperforms the competitors.

AI.tldr: A training-free few-shot framework for prenatal ultrasound anomaly classification and localization using multi-granular prototypes, soft merging, and class-aware refinement.
AI.motivation: Prenatal anomaly classification via ultrasound is challenging due to low prevalence, high heterogeneity, and lack of large annotated datasets. Existing deep learning methods require fine-tuning for new categories, which is impractical in resource-limited clinical settings.
AI.method: Three-component framework: (1) memory bank with multi-granular prototypes for class-level semantics and anomaly characteristics, (2) prototype-driven soft merging for discriminative feature aggregation, (3) class-aware refinement using prototype consistency for improved predictions.
AI.result: Validated on multi-center dataset with 1,149 cases, 2,357 images, and 9 categories; outperformed competitors.
AI.conclusion: The proposed training-free framework effectively addresses few-shot prenatal US anomaly classification and localization without requiring fine-tuning, showing strong performance on a large multi-center dataset.
